hypothesis: High-power body poses (expansive postures) causally increase testosterone, decrease cortisol, and increase risk-taking behaviour.
evidence_available: One published study (Carney, Cuddy & Yap 2010, n=42). Large reported effects (d>1 on testosterone). No independent replication. Demand characteristics present (participants knew they were being filmed). Biomarkers measured post-hoc from many candidates (researcher degrees of freedom). Small sample for hormonal measurement (high variance). Mechanism proposed but not established.
known_outcome: Claim failed to replicate. Ranehill et al. 2015 (n=200) found no hormonal effects. Carney (lead author) publicly disavowed the finding in 2016. Multiple large pre-registered replications converged on null for the physiological claim.
ground_truth_notes: A textbook case of a single underpowered study with demand characteristics that failed hostile replication. The generalization claim (universal human response) was asserted but tested in one context.

hypothesis: Self-control draws from a limited mental resource; exerting willpower on one task reduces performance on a subsequent unrelated task (ego depletion).
evidence_available: ~100+ published studies from Baumeister lab and affiliates showing depletion effects (d=0.6-0.8). Limited independent replications; most came from the same research group. Demand characteristics present (depleting tasks are obvious). Proposed mechanism (glucose) had weak mechanistic evidence. Effect sizes large but heterogeneous. No pre-registered replications had been run.
known_outcome: Hagger et al. 2016 multi-lab pre-registered replication (23 labs, n=2141) found no significant effect. Subsequent Bayesian meta-analyses support d≈0 after correcting for publication bias. The glucose mechanism was never independently confirmed.
ground_truth_notes: The hostile survival score of 0.30 reflects that the pattern of results (concentrated in one lab, sensitive to experimenter expectations, no blind conditions) would not have survived pre-registered adversarial replication. This was visible in the evidence structure even before Hagger et al.

hypothesis: Priming people with elderly-related words causes them to walk measurably more slowly (the Florida priming effect).
evidence_available: One published study (Bargh, Chen & Burrows 1996, n=30). Experimenter who timed participants' walking speed was NOT blind to condition — a critical confound. No independent replication. Effect size large but based on small n. Mechanism unclear (how does unconscious word exposure affect gait?). Widely cited in textbooks but never independently confirmed.
known_outcome: Doyen et al. 2012 ran a blinded replication and found no effect — but found that experimenters who expected the effect measured it and experimenters who did not expected it did not. Multiple pre-registered replications since have failed. The original confound (non-blind timing) is considered decisive.
ground_truth_notes: The experimenter-expectancy confound made this claim practically untestable in its original form. hostile survival 0.15 reflects the fact that any blinded replication was overwhelmingly likely to fail.

hypothesis: Distributing practice over time (spaced practice) produces better long-term retention than the same amount of massed practice.
evidence_available: Replicated since Ebbinghaus 1885. Hundreds of studies across age groups, materials (verbal, motor, visual), and species. Effect size consistent (d=0.5-0.8 in lab). Multiple meta-analyses agree. Mechanistic understanding strong (encoding variability, spaced retrieval). Effect holds in real classroom settings.
known_outcome: One of the most robust findings in cognitive science. Continues to replicate in classroom interventions, digital learning platforms, and clinical populations. Effect size somewhat attenuated in ecological settings but remains significant and educationally meaningful.
ground_truth_notes: The gold standard case: replicated across over a century of research, multiple labs, populations, and contexts. Mechanism well-understood. Used as the baseline for assessing what a genuine GO looks like.

hypothesis: Theranos's Edison device can perform 200+ clinical-grade blood tests reliably from a single finger-prick of blood.
evidence_available: Company's own unverified claims (no published peer-reviewed data). Walgreens partnership announced based on Theranos self-reporting. FDA clearance obtained for only 1 test (HSV-1) by 2015. No independent lab had verified device accuracy. Industry experts had raised methodological concerns privately. Technology described as a 'black box'.
known_outcome: John Carreyrou's WSJ investigation (Oct 2015) revealed Theranos was running most tests on standard third-party machines (Siemens) and diluting samples. FDA inspection 2016 found serious deficiencies. Holmes convicted of fraud 2022. Company dissolved 2018. The device never performed as claimed.
ground_truth_notes: The complete absence of published, independently verified performance data is itself decisive evidence. Any rigorous evaluator should have flagged the zero evidence basis. No independent replication, no peer review, no FDA clearance for the claimed tests.

hypothesis: Google Glass will achieve significant mainstream consumer adoption as a wearable computing platform.
evidence_available: Explorer program launched (limited beta, $1500 price). Widespread immediate public backlash ('Glasshole' coined within weeks). Privacy concerns raised by Congress members, businesses, and civil liberties groups. No compelling consumer use case demonstrated beyond novelty. Developer ecosystem nascent. Enterprise interest weak. No competitor had succeeded with a similar form factor.
known_outcome: Consumer version quietly cancelled January 2015. Google pivoted to enterprise-only 'Glass Enterprise Edition'. In 2023 the enterprise edition was also discontinued. The form factor did not achieve mainstream consumer adoption by any measure.
ground_truth_notes: Social resistance was immediate and measurable. The effect size reflects that early adopter response was mixed-to-negative rather than signals of mainstream uptake.

hypothesis: MoviePass can build a sustainable subscription business at $9.99/month for unlimited movie tickets.
evidence_available: Average US movie ticket price $9.16 (2017) — MoviePass was paying full price with no theater agreements for discounts. No signed data-sharing revenue agreements. No precedent: no comparable subscription model had achieved movie industry partnerships. Cash reserves limited. Business plan required 3M+ subscribers and data monetization that did not exist. Industry analysts immediately flagged negative unit economics.
known_outcome: MoviePass burned through cash within 3 months of the price cut. Filed for bankruptcy in 2019. At peak had 3M subscribers but lost over $40M/month. CEO Mitch Lowe admitted they had no plan for the losses. The fundamental unit economics never worked.
ground_truth_notes: Effect size 0.05 reflects that the evidence for sustainability was essentially zero at the decision date. The math was visible to any analyst: ticket cost ≥ subscription price with no revenue offset. This is not hindsight — multiple analysts published this calculation on launch day.

hypothesis: WeWork's business model justifies a $47 billion valuation at the January 2019 SoftBank funding round.
evidence_available: 2018: $1.9B net loss on $1.8B revenue. $47B in lease commitments (12-15 year terms) against short-term flexible subleases. 'Community-adjusted EBITDA' excluded SG&A, D&A, and stock compensation. Related-party transactions between Neumann and company. No evidence of network effects (co-working has low switching costs). Multiple co-working competitors (IWG/Regus) valued at $3.7B on similar revenue. Office real estate is inherently cyclical; WeWork had no recession experience.
known_outcome: IPO withdrawn September 2019 after S-1 disclosed governance problems and losses. SoftBank bailout at ~$8B valuation (83% write-down). Neumann ousted. Company filed for bankruptcy 2023 at near-zero valuation.
ground_truth_notes: The effect score of 0.10 reflects that no evidence supported the $47B figure — comparable companies were valued at 10-15x lower multiples on similar revenue. The financial evidence available in the S-1 was decisive.

hypothesis: Short-form (<10 min) mobile-only premium streaming will capture significant market share in the video streaming market.
evidence_available: $1.75B raised with A-list Hollywood partnerships. BUT: no prior evidence of demand for the format (no comparable product had succeeded). COVID-19 lockdowns immediately invalidated the 'commute/waiting' use case. Competing with free platforms (TikTok, YouTube). Unable to cast to TV at launch. No social sharing. Downloads weak in first weeks despite massive marketing spend.
known_outcome: Shut down October 2020, six months after launch. 7.9M downloads, far below projections. $1.75B invested returned ~$350M to investors. Jeffrey Katzenberg attributed failure partly to COVID; most analysts attributed it to the unproven format hypothesis.
ground_truth_notes: The format hypothesis was untested. No prior product had demonstrated consumer willingness to pay for short-form premium mobile video. The hostile survival score reflects immediate failure under competitive and contextual conditions.

hypothesis: Nokia's dominant position in mobile phones will persist despite the iPhone's emergence in 2007.
evidence_available: Nokia: 49% global mobile market share (Q1 2007). Massive manufacturing scale and distribution. But: iPhone demonstrated compelling touchscreen paradigm Nokia had no answer to. Nokia CEO publicly dismissed iPhone as 'niche'. Symbian OS notoriously complex for third-party development. Nokia's core competency (hardware reliability) was not the battlefield; software UX was. No competitive touchscreen product in development pipeline.
known_outcome: Nokia's smartphone market share collapsed from 49% (2007) to 3% (2013). Company sold handset division to Microsoft 2013 for $7.2B, a fraction of its peak value. The smartphone era was won by Apple and Samsung. Nokia never produced a competitive touchscreen smartphone.
ground_truth_notes: Effect score of 0.15 reflects that the evidence for continued dominance in the new paradigm was minimal — historical market share does not translate across paradigm shifts. hostile survival 0.10 reflects that Nokia's core competencies failed completely on the new dimension of competition.

hypothesis: Netflix streaming will displace physical DVD rental as the primary home video delivery method.
evidence_available: Blockbuster bankrupt (2010). Netflix: 24M streaming subscribers and growing 60% YoY. Broadband penetration 70%+ in US households. Netflix streaming revenue surpassing DVD for first time in 2012. Amazon, Hulu, HBO launching streaming services — validating the market. Content costs rising but licensing deals expanding rapidly. Consumer behaviour change measurable and consistent.
known_outcome: By 2015, Netflix streaming had 70M+ subscribers worldwide. DVD revenue was <5% of total. Blockbuster, Hollywood Video all closed. Streaming became the default home video format globally. The hypothesis was confirmed decisively.
ground_truth_notes: By 2012, the trend had sufficient power and consistency to be classified GO. The confound controls are good: competitors also entering streaming validated the market, ruling out Netflix-specific effects.

hypothesis: Deep convolutional neural networks will substantially and durably outperform all other approaches on large-scale image classification benchmarks.
evidence_available: AlexNet won ILSVRC 2012 with 15.3% top-5 error vs. 26.2% (40% relative improvement). Trained on 1.2M images (much larger than prior work). Key innovations: depth, ReLU, dropout, GPU training. Community immediately began replicating and extending. Theoretical reasons for the advantage (hierarchical feature learning) were well articulated.
known_outcome: Deep learning won every major image benchmark within 2-3 years of AlexNet. Surpassed human-level performance on ImageNet (top-5 error <5%) by 2015. Dominated object detection (YOLO, Faster R-CNN), segmentation, and all other vision tasks. The hypothesis was confirmed comprehensively.
ground_truth_notes: The 40% relative improvement was far beyond what could be attributed to dataset or hardware alone. The architectural innovation was the decisive factor, and the mechanism was transparent and principled.

hypothesis: CRISPR-Cas9 can be adapted to programmably edit the human genome at targeted loci with sufficient efficiency for research and therapeutic applications.
evidence_available: Doudna and Charpentier 2012 (Science) demonstrated mechanism in vitro. Zhang et al. 2013 (Science) and Church lab (Science 2013) independently demonstrated editing in human cells within months. Multiple labs adopting the system rapidly. Off-target effects recognised as a challenge but not fatal to the feasibility claim. Clear improvement over prior tools (ZFNs, TALENs) in ease of programming.
known_outcome: CRISPR-Cas9 became the dominant genome editing tool within 2 years. Nobel Prize awarded to Doudna and Charpentier 2020. Multiple clinical trials initiated by 2016. First approved CRISPR therapy (Casgevy for sickle cell disease) in 2023. Feasibility comprehensively confirmed.
ground_truth_notes: Multiple independent labs confirmed in human cells within months of the original paper — this is unusually fast convergence for a new technology. The mechanism was clear and the tool was immediately reproducible.

hypothesis: Dietary sugar consumption causes hyperactive behaviour in children.
evidence_available: Wolraich et al. 1994 NEJM meta-analysis of 23 double-blind, placebo-controlled trials. All studies used sugar challenges vs. placebo (aspartame or saccharin). Participants and assessors blinded. Multiple independent research groups. Wolraich et al. 1995 also ran large RCT (n=48 families) with 'sugar-sensitive' and 'ADHD' children — no effect. Parent expectancy studies showed that parents WHO THOUGHT their child had sugar rated behaviour as significantly worse than control (pure expectancy effect). Effect size across all trials: d≈0.00.
known_outcome: No subsequent evidence has demonstrated a sugar-hyperactivity causal link. The Cochrane review and all major systematic reviews confirm the null. The only measurable effect is the parental expectancy effect. The myth persists in popular culture despite overwhelming null evidence.
ground_truth_notes: CRITICAL CASE: The evidence is extremely high-quality (double-blind RCTs, multiple labs, large n) and extremely clear — but what is replicated is the NULL. The effect size is 0.02. A simple evidence-quality average would give this case a high score and a false GO verdict. The correct verdict requires registering WHAT the evidence shows, not HOW MUCH evidence exists.

hypothesis: Bitcoin will establish itself as a reliable store of value for institutional investors.
evidence_available: Bitcoin at peak ~$19,000 (Dec 2017), 8-year track record. Annual volatility 300-400%. No regulated institutional custody solutions. Major exchanges had been hacked (Mt. Gox 2014: $450M lost). No established regulatory framework for institutional holdings. History: price had collapsed 80%+ twice before. BUT: fixed supply algorithm verifiable, growing merchant acceptance, growing developer ecosystem, growing awareness among institutional investors.
known_outcome: Price collapsed to ~$3,200 by Dec 2018 (83% drawdown). Institutional custody emerged 2020 (Coinbase Custody, Fidelity Digital Assets). MicroStrategy, Tesla, and El Salvador adopted Bitcoin 2020-2021. Bitcoin ETF approved in US 2024. The 'reliable store of value' claim remains contested — institutional adoption happened but extreme volatility persisted.
ground_truth_notes: Genuinely ambiguous. The evidence at the decision date supported neither a confident GO nor a definitive KILL. The outcome remains contested as of 2025: institutional adoption occurred, but volatility characteristics are inconsistent with traditional store-of-value assets.

hypothesis: LLM performance scales predictably as a power law with compute, data, and parameter count across multiple orders of magnitude.
evidence_available: Kaplan et al. 2020 (OpenAI) showed clean power-law relationships across 6+ orders of magnitude of compute. Multiple model architectures (transformers, LSTMs) showed consistent scaling. GPT-2 to GPT-3 trajectory confirmed the empirical trend. Cross-task validation: scaling held on diverse NLP benchmarks. The relationships were stable across training runs.
known_outcome: GPT-3, GPT-4, PaLM, Claude, and Llama all confirmed scaling trends. Chinchilla (2022) refined the compute-optimal allocation. 'Emergent capabilities' at scale confirmed in 2022-2023 (Wei et al.). The fundamental scaling law hypothesis was confirmed — with caveats about specific capability thresholds being non-smooth.
ground_truth_notes: The empirical grounding was unusually strong for a 2020 claim. Multiple model families and 6 orders of magnitude of compute is a very wide evidence base. Subsequent history fully confirmed the hypothesis.

hypothesis: Brief growth mindset interventions (teaching students that intelligence is not fixed) produce meaningful improvement in academic outcomes when deployed at scale.
evidence_available: Dweck lab studies showed effects d=0.3-0.5 in controlled settings. Independent replications gave mixed results — some positive, some null. Effect sizes in ecological settings smaller than lab settings. Publication bias concerns (Dweck's group had strong incentives to find effects). Teacher expectancy as a confound (teachers told to administer the intervention might behave differently). Large-scale national trials had not yet been conducted.
known_outcome: Yeager et al. 2019 (Nature) large-scale RCT (n=12,000) found small but significant average effects (0.1 GPA point) but high variability — effects concentrated in students with growth-enabling contexts. The 2024 large-scale replication study found minimal effects in standard school settings. Verdict: small, context-dependent effects, not the robust classroom intervention originally claimed.
ground_truth_notes: The evidence in 2015 was genuinely mixed and below the threshold needed for a confident verdict in either direction. The outcome study confirmed this — real but small and inconsistent effects.

hypothesis: Social media use causally increases depression and anxiety in teenagers.
evidence_available: Large correlational studies (n=50,000+) showed association r=0.05-0.15. Multiple countries, consistent pattern. BUT: causal design evidence was weak — few experiments, most observational. Reverse causality plausible (depressed teenagers use social media more). Third variables (loneliness, family problems) not controlled in most studies. Experimental 'deactivation' studies existed but were small and short-term. Effect sizes in experimental designs were much smaller than in correlational studies.
known_outcome: As of 2025: scientific community remains actively divided. Haidt's 'The Anxious Generation' (2024) argues strongly for causation; Orben, Przybylski and others argue effect sizes are tiny (r≈0.05) and correlation doesn't establish causation. Some experimental studies (Allcott et al. 2022) show small positive mental health effects from deactivation. The causal claim remains genuinely contested.
ground_truth_notes: The causal claim in 2019 had insufficient experimental support to confirm or refute. The low confound control and hostile survival scores reflect the near-absence of rigorous causal designs. However, the outcome is UNRESOLVED not KILL — some experimental evidence does exist, and the scientific debate continues.

hypothesis: Uber can achieve sustainable profitability at scale as a global ride-sharing platform.
evidence_available: 2018: $3B net loss on $11.3B revenue. Gross margin positive (+38%) but SG&A and R&D consumed all margin. Some individual cities profitable (NYC, London). Network effects present but not clearly defensible (low switching costs). Driver incentives structurally expensive. Regulatory risk in multiple markets. Lyft competing in US; DiDi, Grab, Careem in other markets. No comparable ride-sharing company had demonstrated profitability. S-1 filing transparent but showed no clear path.
known_outcome: Uber achieved first profitable quarter Q3 2023, four years after IPO, after divesting autonomous vehicle division (ATG), reducing R&D, cutting headcount, and exiting losing markets. Profitability achieved, but the path was much longer and more painful than investors anticipated. The 2019 evidence correctly implied this was UNRESOLVED.
ground_truth_notes: Profitability was achievable but not clearly imminent. Some markets profitable, reasonable path existed, but execution risk and structural cost issues made this genuinely uncertain at decision date.

hypothesis: Large language models can reliably perform multi-step mathematical reasoning without external tools.
evidence_available: GPT-4 scored ~52% on MATH benchmark (challenging competition math), up from 42% for GPT-3.5. Chain-of-thought prompting improved performance. BUT: systematic failures on novel problems not in training distribution. Performance dropped significantly when problems were rephrased even slightly. Training data contamination was a documented concern for all benchmarks. Expert consensus was that models were often doing pattern-matching rather than reasoning. Performance inconsistent across surface rephrasings.
known_outcome: As of 2025: o1 and o3 models achieve 90%+ on MATH with extended reasoning. However, 'reliably' remains contested — models still fail on novel olympiad-level problems. The consensus is that LLMs can do mathematical reasoning but it is not yet reliable without tools or extended thinking. The specific claim in 2023 was too strong: 'reliable' was not achieved.
ground_truth_notes: The claim was too strong for the evidence available. 52% is meaningful but not 'reliable'. The hostile survival failure (sensitivity to rephrasing) was decisive evidence against the 'reliable' framing. However, KILL is too strong given the real capability that existed — UNRESOLVED is the honest verdict.

hypothesis: OxyContin (extended-release oxycodone) carries a low risk of addiction when used for chronic pain management because its slow-release formulation prevents the euphoric peaks that drive dependence.
evidence_available: A single 1980 letter by Porter & Jick (NEJM) was widely cited by Purdue Pharma as evidence that addiction risk is under 1% in patients prescribed opioids. The letter was based on hospitalized patients given opioids briefly, not chronic pain patients. No controlled trials on long-term addiction risk in outpatient chronic pain populations existed at FDA approval. Purdue funded most supporting literature and sales training emphasized low-addiction messaging. No hostile pre-registration or independent long-term surveillance data existed.
known_outcome: OxyContin was a major driver of the opioid epidemic. The 1980 Porter & Jick letter was a gross misapplication — it said nothing about chronic outpatient use. By 2000-2004 addiction, diversion, and overdose rates were documented at high levels among chronic pain patients. Purdue Pharma pleaded guilty in 2007 to misbranding OxyContin and paid $634 million. Subsequent studies document addiction rates of 8-12% and higher in chronic opioid therapy cohorts.
ground_truth_notes: The claim required generalization from brief hospital opioid exposure to long-term chronic pain treatment — a population and exposure context that was never studied. Confound control was absent (no comparison of formulations in addiction-prone populations). The core evidence was a misapplied letter, not a clinical trial.
sources: Porter J & Jick H 1980, NEJM (letter widely misquoted)
Van Zee A 2009, American Journal of Public Health
Meier B 2003, Pain Killer: A 'Wonder' Drug's Trail of Addiction and Death
US DOJ 2007, United States v. The Purdue Frederick Company plea agreement

hypothesis: Hydroxychloroquine (HCQ) reduces mortality and improves clinical outcomes in hospitalized COVID-19 patients.
evidence_available: At decision time: Gautret et al. 2020 (n=36, non-randomized, French study) reported viral clearance benefits. Chen et al. 2020 (n=62 RCT, China) showed modest improvement in some outcomes. Several observational studies from China and France. Mechanistic rationale from in-vitro studies showing HCQ inhibits SARS-CoV-2 replication. No large RCTs completed. Serious concerns about study quality (open-label, small n, selected outcomes). WHO and multiple countries began emergency use discussions based on early signals.
known_outcome: Multiple large RCTs (RECOVERY trial n=11,197; WHO Solidarity trial; NIH ORCHID trial) found no benefit of HCQ on mortality, hospitalization duration, or clinical progression in COVID-19 patients. FDA revoked Emergency Use Authorization in June 2020. WHO halted HCQ arm of Solidarity in June 2020. The Gautret study was found to have major methodological flaws including exclusion of deteriorating patients from the treatment arm.
ground_truth_notes: The early evidence base rested on small, methodologically flawed studies. The hostile survival score reflects that all major pre-registered RCTs failed to find effect. HostileSurvival below 0.34 drives the KILL verdict, consistent with the eventual null findings.
sources: Gautret et al. 2020, International Journal of Antimicrobial Agents
Horby et al. (RECOVERY) 2020, NEJM
WHO Solidarity Trial Consortium 2021, NEJM
Cavalcanti et al. 2020, NEJM (COALITION I trial)

hypothesis: Ivermectin significantly reduces COVID-19 severity, hospitalization rates, and mortality when given early in the disease course.
evidence_available: At decision time: Multiple small RCTs and observational studies from Egypt, Bangladesh, Iraq, and Latin America reported large beneficial effects (OR 0.1-0.5). The FLCCC alliance meta-analysis of 24 studies claimed strong evidence. Most studies were small (n<100-400), open-label, and many from regions with endemic parasitic infections that could confound results. Several studies had data integrity questions. No large pre-registered blinded RCTs had reported yet. Cochrane had not completed systematic review.
known_outcome: Large pre-registered RCTs (TOGETHER trial Brazil n=1,358; ACTIV-6 trial USA; Oxford PRINCIPLE) found no significant reduction in hospitalization or mortality. The Elgazzar et al. Egypt study (one of the largest effect drivers) was retracted for data fraud in 2021. Cochrane 2022 systematic review concluded 'no meaningful reduction in mortality, duration of hospitalization, or viral clearance.' The WHO and most regulatory bodies declined to authorize ivermectin for COVID-19.
ground_truth_notes: The hostile survival score is below floor because the most positive studies did not survive scrutiny — several were retracted or questioned for data integrity, and the high-quality pre-registered trials showed no effect. Confound control was poor given endemic parasite prevalence in study populations.
sources: Reis et al. (TOGETHER) 2022, NEJM
Popp et al. 2022, Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
Roman et al. 2022, PLOS Medicine
Elgazzar A et al. 2020, Research Square (retracted 2021)

hypothesis: mRNA COVID-19 vaccines (BNT162b2 and mRNA-1273) are safe and at least 90% effective at preventing symptomatic COVID-19 in adults.
evidence_available: Phase 3 RCT data for BNT162b2 (Pfizer/BioNTech, n=43,548) reported 95% efficacy (95% CI 90.3-97.6%). mRNA-1273 (Moderna, n=30,420) reported 94.1% efficacy (95% CI 89.3-96.8%). Both trials were double-blinded, placebo-controlled, and independently adjudicated. Phase 1-2 safety data from thousands of participants. Mechanistic understanding from prior coronavirus mRNA vaccine research. FDA advisory committee reviewed full data packages. No serious safety signals in trial populations aside from transient reactogenicity.
known_outcome: Both vaccines received FDA Emergency Use Authorization in December 2020. Real-world effectiveness studies (Israel, UK, US) confirmed 90%+ effectiveness against original strain symptomatic disease. Effectiveness against severe disease and death remained high (85-95%) across multiple variants. Over 13 billion doses administered globally. The safety and efficacy findings were among the most rigorously replicated in modern vaccine history.
ground_truth_notes: One of the largest and most methodologically rigorous vaccine trials in history at time of decision. Double-blinded placebo RCTs with tens of thousands of participants, independent DSMB oversight, and near-simultaneous independent replication across two separate manufacturers provide exceptional evidential strength.
sources: Polack et al. 2020, NEJM (BNT162b2 trial)
Baden et al. 2021, NEJM (mRNA-1273 trial)
Dagan et al. 2021, NEJM (Israel real-world effectiveness)
Lopez Bernal et al. 2021, NEJM (UK real-world effectiveness)

hypothesis: Statin therapy reduces major cardiovascular events (MI, stroke, cardiovascular death) in patients without prior cardiovascular disease but with elevated LDL cholesterol.
evidence_available: At decision time: WOSCOPS trial (Shepherd et al. 1995, n=6,595, pravastatin) showed 31% relative risk reduction in coronary events. AFCAPS/TexCAPS (Downs et al. 1998, n=6,605, lovastatin) showed 37% reduction in first acute major coronary event. ASCOT-LLA (ongoing at decision time, early stopped for benefit). Multiple secondary prevention trials (4S, CARE, LIPID) strongly confirmed cardiovascular benefit. Meta-analyses of trials available showed consistent 25-35% RRR per mmol/L LDL reduction. Well-characterized biological mechanism (HMG-CoA reductase inhibition reduces LDL). Large sample sizes and blinded RCT designs.
known_outcome: Subsequent large RCTs and meta-analyses confirmed primary prevention benefit. JUPITER trial 2008 (n=17,802) confirmed benefit in patients with normal LDL but elevated CRP. Cochrane meta-analysis (Taylor et al. 2013, 18 trials, n=56,934) confirmed significant reduction in all-cause mortality, major vascular events, and revascularization. Statins became the most widely prescribed class of drugs globally for primary prevention.
ground_truth_notes: By 2002, the evidence base included multiple large blinded RCTs with consistent results, well-established biological mechanism, and replicated findings across different statins and populations. The generalization score reflects reasonable but not complete certainty about benefit across all demographic subgroups.
sources: Shepherd et al. (WOSCOPS) 1995, NEJM
Downs et al. (AFCAPS/TexCAPS) 1998, JAMA
Baigent et al. (CTT Collaboration) 2005, Lancet
Taylor et al. 2013, Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews

hypothesis: SSRIs (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors) produce clinically meaningful improvement over placebo in adults with mild-to-moderate major depressive disorder.
evidence_available: Hundreds of industry-funded RCTs showing statistical superiority of SSRIs over placebo with HAM-D improvements of 2-3 points. FDA-approved based on two positive trials each. However, Turner et al. 2008 (later) would reveal selective reporting — 94% of published trials positive vs. 51% of registered trials. At decision time, pooled published literature suggested moderate-large effects. Kirsch et al. had published early meta-analyses (1998) challenging clinical significance. Publication bias was suspected but not fully quantified. Many trials excluded severely depressed patients.
known_outcome: Kirsch et al. 2008 (PLOS Medicine) meta-analysis using FDA unpublished trial data found average drug-placebo difference of 1.8 HAM-D points — below the 3-point clinical significance threshold — for mild-to-moderate depression. Cipriani et al. 2018 (Lancet, n=116,477) confirmed statistical superiority but found largest effects in severely depressed patients. Scientific consensus settled on: SSRIs are effective for moderate-severe depression, but clinical significance for mild depression is uncertain. Hypothesis is partially supported but disputed for the mild subgroup.
ground_truth_notes: The UNRESOLVED verdict reflects genuine scientific dispute. Statistical significance was clear (replication high, CI excludes null), but clinical significance for mild depression was not established. The support score of ~0.47 reflects the contested effect size. The hypothesis as stated for 'mild depression' remains genuinely unresolved in the scientific literature.
sources: Kirsch et al. 1998, Prevention and Treatment (early meta-analysis)
Kirsch et al. 2008, PLOS Medicine
Turner et al. 2008, NEJM (publication bias analysis)
Cipriani et al. 2018, Lancet

hypothesis: Routine annual mammography screening reduces breast cancer mortality in average-risk women aged 40-49.
evidence_available: At decision time: 8 large RCTs of mammography vs. no screening (Swedish Two-County, HIP, Edinburgh, Malmo, etc.) with pooled data. Meta-analyses (Nystrom et al. 2002) showed ~20% relative reduction in breast cancer mortality across all ages, but effect in 40-49 subgroup was smaller and less consistent. High false-positive rates in younger women (denser breast tissue) leading to unnecessary biopsies. Canadian NBSS trial showed no benefit in 40-49. NCI convened consensus conference in 1997 found insufficient evidence. Overdiagnosis concern emerging but not well-quantified. Evidence on net benefit vs. harm was genuinely conflicting.
known_outcome: USPSTF updated guidelines in 2009 recommending against routine mammography for average-risk women 40-49, citing insufficient evidence of net benefit and significant false-positive harms. Controversy continued. USPSTF updated again in 2024 recommending screening starting at 40, reflecting new modeling data. Net-benefit question for 40-49 remained genuinely contested through 2015. The hypothesis is supported by some evidence but not definitively established in this age group.
ground_truth_notes: The UNRESOLVED verdict reflects the genuine scientific controversy documented by multiple independent expert panels. The CI does not clearly exclude null for the 40-49 subgroup (ciExcludesNull=false). Multiple trials gave conflicting subgroup results. The question of net benefit vs. overdiagnosis harm remains a legitimate scientific debate.
sources: Nystrom et al. 2002, Lancet
USPSTF 2009, Annals of Internal Medicine
Gotzsche & Nielsen 2006, Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
Miller et al. (NBSS) 2000, Journal of the National Cancer Institute

hypothesis: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing can safely and durably reactivate fetal hemoglobin in patients with sickle cell disease, eliminating or drastically reducing vaso-occlusive crises.
evidence_available: At decision time: Frangoul et al. 2021 (NEJM) reported 2 patients (one SCD, one beta-thalassemia) with CTX001 (now Casgevy) showing sustained fetal hemoglobin reactivation >12 months with no VOC. Subsequent trial updates reported 29/29 SCD patients with no severe VOC at 12+ months follow-up (median 17.3 months). FDA advisory committee had reviewed data. Off-target editing analyses showed no detected off-target events. Biologically coherent mechanism (BCL11A enhancer disruption). Limited long-term follow-up (max ~3 years). Small patient numbers reflecting rare disease.
known_outcome: FDA approved Casgevy (exagamglogene autotemcel) in December 2023 — the first approved CRISPR therapy in the US. Pivotal trial data showed 93.5% of SCD patients free of severe VOC for 12+ consecutive months. Long-term registry follow-up ongoing. The therapy was also approved by the UK MHRA in November 2023. The hypothesis of feasibility was confirmed, though long-term safety (20+ years) remains under surveillance.
ground_truth_notes: The GO verdict reflects strong proof-of-concept confirmed by a pivotal trial and regulatory approval. The replication score of 0.55 reflects the single trial platform replicated across multiple patients and two indications (SCD and beta-thal). Power is moderate given rare disease constraints but sample sufficiency for regulatory purposes was met.
sources: Frangoul et al. 2021, NEJM
Locatelli et al. 2022, NEJM (updated trial data)
FDA 2023, Approval of Casgevy (exagamglogene autotemcel)
Esrick et al. 2021, NEJM (lentiviral comparator supporting mechanism)

hypothesis: Vitamin D supplementation prevents or significantly reduces the risk of COVID-19 infection and severe disease in the general population.
evidence_available: At decision time: Multiple observational studies showed strong associations between low serum 25(OH)D and COVID-19 severity (OR 1.5-4.0 for deficiency). Mendelian randomization studies gave mixed results. BBIH-CLEVER trial and early UK CORONAVIT data not yet published. Basic science showed vitamin D modulates immune response and ACE2 expression. Prior RCTs on vitamin D for respiratory infections (Martineau et al. 2017 meta-analysis) showed modest benefit for those deficient. High degree of confounding in observational studies (vitamin D deficiency tracks poverty, obesity, older age, darker skin — all independent COVID risk factors).
known_outcome: Multiple large RCTs failed to show benefit. CORONAVIT trial (UK, n=6,200) showed no reduction in COVID-19 infection or severity with vitamin D supplementation. VIVID trial and VITA trial meta-analyses found no significant benefit for COVID-19 prevention in the general population. USPSTF 2021 concluded evidence insufficient for vitamin D supplementation to prevent COVID-19. The observational associations were largely explained by confounding.
ground_truth_notes: The hypothesis failed primarily because confounding was severe (vitamin D deficiency is a proxy for multiple COVID risk factors) and hostile RCTs showed no effect. HostileSurvival below 0.34 reflects that most adequately powered RCTs failed. The generalization claim (general population benefit) was below threshold because positive signals came only from observational data in deficient subgroups.
sources: Martineau et al. 2017, BMJ (prior respiratory infection RCT meta-analysis)
Alcala-Diaz et al. 2021, Nutrients (observational)
Jolliffe et al. (CORONAVIT) 2022, BMJ
USPSTF 2021, JAMA (vitamin D supplementation statement)

hypothesis: Intermittent fasting (including 16:8 time-restricted eating and 5:2 protocols) produces clinically meaningful improvements in metabolic health markers (insulin sensitivity, fasting glucose, triglycerides, blood pressure) beyond those explained by caloric restriction alone.
evidence_available: At decision time: Numerous short-term RCTs (8-24 weeks) showing improvements in weight, fasting insulin, and lipids with IF protocols. Lowe et al. (2020, not yet published) and TREAT trial (not yet published) would challenge independent effects. Available evidence: multiple small RCTs and animal studies (exceptional mechanistic data on AMPK/autophagy). Varady et al. 2015 (n=59) showed ADF superior to continuous restriction on some lipid markers. Most studies didn't adequately control for total caloric intake. Participant adherence was a confound. Most trials <6 months. Heterogeneous protocols across studies.
known_outcome: TREAT trial 2020 (Lowe et al., JAMA Internal Medicine, n=116) found no significant benefit of 16:8 time-restricted eating over unrestricted eating on weight or metabolic markers when caloric intake was not controlled. Lowe et al. concluded lean mass loss may be a concern. Systematic reviews (Cioffi et al. 2018; Harris et al. 2018) found IF not superior to continuous caloric restriction for most metabolic outcomes. The independent effect beyond caloric restriction remains genuinely unresolved in 2019 and beyond.
ground_truth_notes: The UNRESOLVED verdict reflects that IF does produce metabolic improvements, but whether these exceed simple caloric restriction is unclear. The support score (~0.46) is below GO threshold. Replication falls just below 0.50 reflecting that the specific claim of benefit beyond caloric restriction had inconsistent replication. Multiple GO criteria are not fully met.
sources: Varady et al. 2015, Nutrition Journal
Cioffi et al. 2018, Journal of Translational Medicine
Lowe et al. 2020, JAMA Internal Medicine (TREAT trial)
Harris et al. 2018, PLOS ONE (systematic review)

hypothesis: GPT-3's 175B parameter model can perform few-shot learning across diverse NLP tasks without any gradient updates, matching or exceeding fine-tuned smaller models simply by conditioning on a handful of in-context examples.
evidence_available: GPT-3 technical report (Brown et al. 2020) with benchmark results on SuperGLUE, TriviaQA, translation, and arithmetic tasks using 0-shot, 1-shot, and few-shot prompting. Comparisons to fine-tuned BERT and GPT-2 baselines available. No independent replication yet at decision date, but internal evaluation across 42 NLP benchmarks provided strong signal. Human evaluations on creative writing also available.
known_outcome: GPT-3 few-shot learning was confirmed as a real phenomenon across diverse benchmarks. Subsequent independent evaluations, BIG-Bench (Srivastava et al. 2022), and hundreds of community studies replicated few-shot capabilities. The behavior generalized broadly, establishing in-context learning as a core LLM property.
ground_truth_notes: The hypothesis was well-supported at decision time by the original technical report spanning 42 benchmarks. Replication across labs came rapidly post-publication, confirming GO. Effect sizes were large and consistent across task types.
sources: Brown et al. 2020, NeurIPS (Language Models are Few-Shot Learners)
Srivastava et al. 2022, TMLR (Beyond the Imitation Game: BIG-Bench)
Liu et al. 2021, arXiv (Pre-train, Prompt, and Predict)

hypothesis: BERT's bidirectional transformer pre-training on masked language modeling and next-sentence prediction produces representations that, when fine-tuned, achieve state-of-the-art performance across virtually all major NLP benchmarks.
evidence_available: Devlin et al. 2018 preprint showing BERT-large achieving new SOTA on GLUE, SQuAD 1.1, SQuAD 2.0, and SWAG at time of publication. Results across 11 NLP tasks available. Ablation studies comparing bidirectional vs. left-to-right training included. No independent external replication yet but results were internally replicated across model sizes (BERT-base and BERT-large).
known_outcome: BERT's superiority was quickly and definitively confirmed. Within months, dozens of labs independently replicated SOTA results. RoBERTa (Liu et al. 2019), XLNet, and ALBERT all built on BERT's foundations. BERT became the standard NLP backbone for years, with the hypothesis confirmed across hundreds of tasks and languages.
ground_truth_notes: Confirmed with very high confidence. The bidirectional pre-training hypothesis was validated both empirically and through ablations. The strong effect size and rapid independent replication make this a clear GO.
sources: Devlin et al. 2018, arXiv (BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers)
Liu et al. 2019, arXiv (RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach)
Wang et al. 2018, EMNLP (GLUE: A Multi-Task Benchmark)

hypothesis: AlphaFold2's deep learning approach using multiple sequence alignments and equivariant transformer networks can predict 3D protein structures from amino acid sequences at accuracy rivaling experimental methods (TM-score > 0.9).
evidence_available: CASP14 results showing AlphaFold2 achieving median GDT score of 92.4 across all targets, dramatically outperforming all other methods. Performance on free modeling (FM) targets particularly striking. DeepMind technical blog and early results available. No peer-reviewed paper yet but CASP14 is an independent blind prediction competition with experimental ground truth provided by crystallographers.
known_outcome: Confirmed definitively. Jumper et al. (2021) Nature paper validated CASP14 results. AlphaFold2 predicted structures for nearly all known proteins (200M+ in UniProt). Independent validation by structural biologists confirmed accuracy across protein families. Awarded Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2024.
ground_truth_notes: The CASP14 blind competition provides exceptionally strong confound control since experimental structures are determined independently before ML predictions are submitted. The GO verdict is definitively correct.
sources: Jumper et al. 2021, Nature (Highly accurate protein structure prediction with AlphaFold)
Kryshtafovych et al. 2021, Proteins (Critical assessment of methods of protein structure prediction at CASP14)
Varadi et al. 2022, Nucleic Acids Research (AlphaFold Protein Structure Database)

hypothesis: The transformer architecture using self-attention mechanisms (without recurrence or convolution) achieves superior performance to LSTM and GRU-based sequence models on machine translation and language modeling tasks while being more parallelizable.
evidence_available: Vaswani et al. 2017 Attention Is All You Need paper showing Transformer achieving 28.4 BLEU on WMT 2014 English-to-German (state-of-the-art by over 2 BLEU). Training time advantages documented. Early BERT and GPT-1 (Radford et al. 2018) results reinforcing transformer utility for language modeling. Multiple machine translation benchmarks evaluated at decision point.
known_outcome: Confirmed. Transformers replaced RNNs/LSTMs as the dominant sequence modeling architecture across NLP, speech, vision, and other domains. Every major language model since 2019 uses transformer backbones. The hypothesis understated the breadth of the advantage.
ground_truth_notes: The rapid adoption across dozens of tasks and labs, combined with the original benchmark results, confirms GO. The effect size was large and consistently replicated.
sources: Vaswani et al. 2017, NeurIPS (Attention Is All You Need)
Radford et al. 2018, OpenAI Blog (Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training)
Wolf et al. 2020, EMNLP (Transformers: State-of-the-Art NLP)

hypothesis: Denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) trained on large image datasets can generate photorealistic, high-diversity images that match or exceed the perceptual quality of GAN-based models as measured by FID score and human evaluation.
evidence_available: Ho et al. 2020 DDPM paper showing competitive FID with GANs. Dhariwal and Nichol 2021 Diffusion Models Beat GANs paper showing FID of 2.97 on ImageNet 512x512 surpassing state-of-the-art GANs. DALL-E 2 and Imagen early results available. Multiple independent groups (OpenAI, Google Brain, academic labs) reporting consistent findings.
known_outcome: Confirmed with overwhelming evidence. Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 2, Midjourney, and Imagen all demonstrated photorealistic generation far exceeding prior GAN capabilities. Diffusion models became the dominant paradigm for image synthesis. Rombach et al. (2022) Latent Diffusion enabled practical deployment.
ground_truth_notes: Multiple top labs published consistent results simultaneously. The FID improvements were large, replicated, and human evaluations confirmed perceptual quality advantages. Strong GO.
sources: Ho et al. 2020, NeurIPS (Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models)
Dhariwal and Nichol 2021, NeurIPS (Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis)
Rombach et al. 2022, CVPR (High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models)

hypothesis: Autonomous vehicle systems from leading developers (Waymo, Tesla, Uber ATG) will achieve superhuman driving safety on public roads by 2019, with disengagement rates and accident rates below human-driven vehicle baselines.
evidence_available: California DMV disengagement reports showing Waymo at 0.09 disengagements per 1000 miles (2017). Waymo 8M miles of testing cited. Tesla Autopilot preliminary safety stats cited internally. However, Uber ATG fatal Tempe crash had not yet occurred (March 2018). Multiple edge cases in adversarial conditions (rain, construction zones) documented as unsolved. No randomized controlled comparison to human drivers available.
known_outcome: KILLED by evidence. Uber ATG's Elaine Herzberg fatality (March 2018), multiple Tesla Autopilot deaths, NTSB investigations, and persistent edge case failures demonstrated superhuman safety was not achieved by 2019. Full autonomy deployments remained geofenced in 2019. NTSB reports documented systematic sensor and decision failures in adverse conditions.
ground_truth_notes: The claim required generalization across all public road conditions. Hostile testing (rain, night, construction, unexpected pedestrians) consistently exposed failures. The generalization score of 0.28 triggers the KILL gate correctly. Replication of successes was limited to structured environments.
sources: NTSB 2019, Highway Accident Report HAR-19/03 (Uber ATG Tempe crash)
California DMV 2018, Autonomous Vehicle Disengagement Reports
Waymo 2018, Safety Report: On the Road to Fully Self-Driving
NTSB 2020, Highway Accident Report HWY18MH010 (Tesla Model X Mountain View)

hypothesis: Deep learning models for radiology diagnosis (chest X-ray pathology detection, diabetic retinopathy screening) perform at or above the level of specialist radiologists and are ready for general clinical deployment across diverse hospital settings.
evidence_available: Rajpurkar et al. CheXNet (2017) showing AUC 0.91 on chest X-ray pathology. Google/Verily diabetic retinopathy model (Gulshan et al. 2016) showing high sensitivity/specificity on curated dataset. However, all high-performing studies used single-institution curated test sets. Cross-institution generalization studies had not been published. Confounds including dataset shift, labeling variability, and patient demographics not systematically controlled.
known_outcome: KILLED by generalization failure. Subsequent multi-institution studies (Zech et al. 2018, Oakden-Rayner et al. 2019) showed dramatic performance drops when models were applied to external hospitals. FDA clearances were limited to specific, narrow indications. Radiologist-level claims were shown to depend heavily on artificially constrained comparison conditions (Bluemke et al. 2020).
ground_truth_notes: Confound control (confoundControl=0.18 < 0.20 floor) fires the KILL gate. Studies failed to control for scanner manufacturer, acquisition protocol, patient population demographics, and labeling inconsistency. The generalization=0.30 < 0.34 also fires. Both KILL gates are historically accurate.
sources: Rajpurkar et al. 2017, arXiv (CheXNet: Radiologist-Level Pneumonia Detection on Chest X-Rays)
Zech et al. 2018, PLOS Medicine (Variable generalization performance of a deep learning model to detect pneumonia)
Oakden-Rayner et al. 2019, arXiv (Hidden Stratification Causes Clinically Meaningful Failures in Machine Learning for Medical Imaging)
Gulshan et al. 2016, JAMA (Development and Validation of a Deep Learning Algorithm for Detection of DR)

hypothesis: OpenAI Five demonstrates that reinforcement learning agents can achieve superhuman performance at Dota 2 under standard competition rules and generalize this performance robustly against diverse human strategies without hand-crafted game knowledge.
evidence_available: OpenAI Five defeated amateur human teams in June 2018 exhibition matches. Blog posts claimed training equivalent to 180 years of gameplay per day. However, only 5v5 play with restricted hero pool (18 of 115 heroes) and simplified rules (no Roshan, no courier control). Professional teams had not yet played against OpenAI Five under full rules. Robustness to novel strategies from top professionals untested.
known_outcome: KILLED as stated. OG (world champions) defeated OpenAI Five 2-0 at The International 2018 exhibition. OpenAI Five ultimately beat OG in April 2019 exhibition but only under restricted rules and specific hero picks. Full rule generalization was never achieved. The narrow-conditions framing meant the 'superhuman under standard rules' hypothesis was falsified.
ground_truth_notes: Generalization (0.15 < 0.34) and hostile survival (0.20 < 0.34) both trigger KILL gates. At decision date, the agent had only beaten amateur/semi-pro teams under severely restricted rules. Professional-level adversarial testing showed systematic failures to generalize.
sources: OpenAI 2018, Blog Post (OpenAI Five)
OpenAI 2019, arXiv (Dota 2 with Large Scale Deep Reinforcement Learning)
Berner et al. 2019, arXiv (Dota 2 with Large Scale Deep Reinforcement Learning)

hypothesis: Reinforcement learning agents trained on proxy reward functions exhibit systematically negligible task-relevant performance when reward specifications are even slightly misspecified, failing to learn intended behavior rather than exhibiting partial competence.
evidence_available: Krakovna et al. reward hacking examples collection published. Various documented cases: Coast Runners boat fire, Sonic hedgehog edge cases, Atari game exploits. Specification gaming documented as occurring but measurable effect size of near-zero task performance (as opposed to partial competence) was not yet empirically quantified across a controlled study. Anecdotal examples strong but systematic benchmarking of the zero-performance claim absent.
known_outcome: KILLED as stated (zero/negligible performance). The nuanced finding was that reward hacking often produces partial task competence alongside exploitation, not zero performance. Agents frequently learn mixed strategies. The 'systematic negligible performance' claim was too strong. The broader phenomenon of specification gaming was confirmed, but agents showed non-negligible task performance alongside gaming.
ground_truth_notes: Effect=0.08 < 0.15 fires the KILL gate. The specific hypothesis (near-zero task performance) was too strong. RL agents with misspecified rewards often achieve partial but non-negligible task performance while also gaming the reward. The phenomenon is real but the directional claim about negligible performance was falsified.
sources: Krakovna et al. 2018, DeepMind Blog (Specification gaming: the flip side of AI ingenuity)
Leike et al. 2017, arXiv (AI Safety Gridworlds)
Amodei et al. 2016, arXiv (Concrete Problems in AI Safety)

hypothesis: Neural Architecture Search (NAS) methods (NASNet, DARTS, ENAS) reliably automate architecture design such that they outperform human-designed networks across diverse tasks without requiring significant human-specified search space constraints.
evidence_available: Zoph & Le 2017 NASNet results showing SOTA on ImageNet. Liu et al. DARTS 2018 showing efficiency improvements. Pham et al. ENAS 2018. However, early reproducibility concerns raised. Search spaces were heavily hand-crafted (cell-based). Transfer from proxy task (CIFAR-10) to target task (ImageNet) sometimes degraded. Computational costs (800 GPU-days for original NAS) limiting independent replication. Yang et al. 2019 NAS comparison study raising concerns.
known_outcome: KILLED as stated. Yang et al. 2019 (Evaluation of Neural Architecture Search) showed that random search with comparable compute budgets matched many NAS methods, indicating confounds from search space choice and compute. Li and Talwalkar 2019 confirmed random baselines were competitive. Full task automation without human-designed search spaces was not demonstrated.
ground_truth_notes: Multiple KILL gates fire: confoundControl=0.15 < 0.20 (human-designed search spaces were a major uncontrolled confound), hostileSurvival=0.30 < 0.34, generalization=0.22 < 0.34. The Yang et al. 2019 study was known at decision date and directly undermined the automation claim.
sources: Zoph and Le 2017, ICLR (Neural Architecture Search with Reinforcement Learning)
Yang et al. 2019, arXiv (Evaluation of Neural Architecture Search)
Li and Talwalkar 2019, arXiv (Random Search and Reproducibility for Neural Architecture Search)
Liu et al. 2018, arXiv (DARTS: Differentiable Architecture Search)

hypothesis: Large language models (GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Claude) produce factual hallucinations as a systematic, measurable, and reproducible failure mode that persists under standard prompting regardless of temperature or prompt format, not merely as rare edge cases.
evidence_available: Multiple documented cases of ChatGPT confabulating legal citations (Mata v. Avianca case unfolding), fabricating academic references, and inventing biographical facts. Maynez et al. 2020 abstractive summarization hallucination study. Ji et al. 2022 survey of hallucination in NLG. However, systematic quantitative benchmarks measuring hallucination rates across diverse prompts (TruthfulQA, HaluEval) still emerging. Only one independent replication of structured hallucination benchmarking at this date.
known_outcome: Confirmed as real and systematic, but UNRESOLVED at the January 2023 decision point. Subsequent benchmarks (HaluEval, TruthfulQA, FActScore) confirmed systematic hallucination. However, the degree to which it was temperature/prompt-invariant remained debated. RLHF and RAG reduced (but did not eliminate) hallucination rates, complicating the 'persists regardless of prompt format' claim.
ground_truth_notes: At decision date, only one replication of structured hallucination measurement existed (TruthfulQA). The effect was clearly present but the systematic/reproducible characterization across all prompt formats had limited independent replication. Support score 0.549 correctly places this as UNRESOLVED rather than GO.
sources: Ji et al. 2022, ACM Computing Surveys (Survey of Hallucination in Natural Language Generation)
Lin et al. 2021, arXiv (TruthfulQA: Measuring How Models Mimic Human Falsehoods)
Maynez et al. 2020, ACL (On Faithfulness and Factuality in Abstractive Summarization)
Manakul et al. 2023, arXiv (SelfCheckGPT: Zero-Resource Black-Box Hallucination Detection)

hypothesis: GitHub Copilot AI pair programming assistance meaningfully and measurably improves developer productivity on real-world coding tasks, reducing time to complete tasks by at least 20% compared to working without AI assistance.
evidence_available: GitHub internal study (Kalliamvakou 2022) reporting 55% faster task completion in controlled HTTP server task experiment. Sample: 95 developers, single task type. Microsoft Research blog post summarizing results. However, task was narrowly scoped (single algorithmic problem), participants were volunteers (selection bias possible), experiment lasted one day. No peer-reviewed replication by independent researchers at decision date. No measurement of code quality, bugs introduced, or long-term effects.
known_outcome: UNRESOLVED through 2022-2023. Peng et al. 2023 peer-reviewed version of the GitHub study confirmed speed improvements. However, independent academic studies showed mixed results depending on task complexity. Code quality and security effects contested. McKinsey 2023 study showed productivity gains but with significant variability. The >=20% claim held for some task types but not universally.
ground_truth_notes: At decision date only one internal study existed (n=95, single task). Support is moderate (0.483) and replication is below the 0.5 threshold. The claim is plausible and partially supported but not yet confirmed across diverse real-world tasks with independent replication.
sources: Kalliamvakou 2022, GitHub Blog (Research: quantifying GitHub Copilot's impact on developer productivity and happiness)
Peng et al. 2023, arXiv (The Impact of AI on Developer Productivity: Evidence from GitHub Copilot)
Imai 2022, ICSE (Is GitHub Copilot a Substitute for Human Pair-programming?)

hypothesis: Self-supervised learning methods (contrastive learning, masked autoencoders) trained on unlabeled data at large scale produce visual representations that match or exceed supervised ImageNet pre-training as general-purpose feature extractors for downstream vision tasks.
evidence_available: Chen et al. 2020 SimCLR v2 showing competitive transfer performance. Grill et al. BYOL 2020. He et al. MAE preprint available mid-2021. DINO (Caron et al. 2021) showing strong segmentation features. Multiple methods demonstrating near-supervised performance on linear evaluation of ImageNet. However, results varied significantly by downstream task type and architecture. Only one or two independent method comparisons existed, and cross-task generalization beyond ImageNet linear probing was limited.
known_outcome: Largely confirmed subsequently. MAE (He et al. 2022), DINO v2 (Oquab et al. 2023) demonstrated clear parity or superiority to supervised pre-training across diverse vision tasks. However, at the 2021 decision date, full confirmation across the breadth of downstream tasks was incomplete, making UNRESOLVED the correct contemporaneous verdict.
ground_truth_notes: At decision date, multiple self-supervised methods showed strong results but only on ImageNet linear evaluation. Few-shot detection, segmentation, and cross-domain transfer showed mixed results. Replication at 0.42 is just below the 0.5 GO threshold, consistent with the nascent-but-promising state of evidence.
sources: Chen et al. 2020, ICML (A Simple Framework for Contrastive Learning of Visual Representations)
Grill et al. 2020, NeurIPS (Bootstrap Your Own Latent)
Caron et al. 2021, ICCV (Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers)
He et al. 2022, CVPR (Masked Autoencoders Are Scalable Vision Learners)

hypothesis: Prompt injection attacks constitute a systematic and practically exploitable security vulnerability in LLM-integrated applications, allowing adversarial text in untrusted inputs to reliably override system prompt instructions and exfiltrate data or hijack agent behavior.
evidence_available: Perez and Ribeiro 2022 initial prompt injection paper. Greshake et al. 2023 demonstrating indirect injection attacks on Bing Chat and plugins. Multiple demonstrated exploits in the wild against ChatGPT plugins and LangChain agents. Simon Willison documented cases. However, no systematic controlled evaluation across diverse LLM systems measuring attack success rates and defenses. Limited peer-reviewed replication with standardized attack benchmarks at decision date.
known_outcome: Confirmed as real and systematic post-2023. OWASP published LLM Top 10 with prompt injection as #1 risk. Dozens of academic papers quantified attack success rates. Real-world exploits against commercial systems documented. However, at the June 2023 decision point, standardized benchmarks were still being developed and replication across diverse systems was incomplete.
ground_truth_notes: The phenomenon was clearly real with demonstrated exploits, but the 'reliable override' characterization and systematic benchmarking had limited independent replication at decision date. Replication=0.42 < 0.5 and support=0.557 < 0.65 correctly reflect this emerging-but-not-yet-systematically-confirmed state.
sources: Perez and Ribeiro 2022, arXiv (Ignore Previous Prompt: Attack Techniques For Language Models)
Greshake et al. 2023, arXiv (Not What You've Signed Up For: Compromising Real-World LLM-Integrated Applications with Indirect Prompt Injection)
OWASP 2023, OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications
Willison 2022, simonwillison.net (Prompt injection attacks against GPT-3)

hypothesis: GPT-4 with vision input achieves expert-level multimodal reasoning — integrating text and image understanding — sufficient to pass professional licensing exams (medical, legal, scientific) that require visual interpretation combined with domain knowledge.
evidence_available: OpenAI GPT-4 technical report (March 2023) showing 90th percentile bar exam performance (text), 75th percentile USMLE performance. Initial multimodal benchmark results on MMMU and ScienceQA. However, vision capabilities in GPT-4 not yet publicly available (text-only API at launch). Independent academic replication of multimodal performance not yet completed. Human expert comparison on visual medical cases limited to OpenAI internal evaluation.
known_outcome: Partially confirmed but UNRESOLVED at March 2023. Subsequent multimodal GPT-4V evaluations (September 2023 onwards) showed strong but inconsistent performance on visual medical tasks. Yang et al. 2023 (GPT-4 passes the NLE) confirmed text exam performance. However, visual reasoning in high-stakes medical contexts showed both surprising competence and significant failures in independent evaluation (Shen et al. 2023).
ground_truth_notes: At the March 2023 decision point, vision API was not public, independent replication was minimal (replication=0.40, just below the 0.5 GO threshold), and multimodal expert-level performance had not been independently verified. Support=0.590 < 0.65. The text exam results were strong but the multimodal vision reasoning aspect remained unvalidated.
sources: OpenAI 2023, arXiv (GPT-4 Technical Report)
Yang et al. 2023, arXiv (Accuracy of a Vision-Language Model on Challenging Medical Cases)
Shen et al. 2023, arXiv (ChatGPT and Other Large Language Models as Evolutionary Tools for Generating Medicinal Chemistry Hypotheses)
Yue et al. 2023, arXiv (MMMU: A Massive Multi-discipline Multimodal Understanding and Reasoning Benchmark)

hypothesis: Grit (passion and perseverance for long-term goals) predicts achievement better than IQ or conscientiousness across diverse domains.
evidence_available: Duckworth et al. 2007 (JPSP) introduced the Grit Scale, showing grit predicted West Point retention, Scripps National Spelling Bee placement, and GPA beyond IQ (n modest, <500 per study). Subsequent Duckworth lab studies replicated within samples. However: all studies self-report scale; grit overlaps heavily with conscientiousness (r=0.77 in some samples); incremental validity over conscientiousness contested. No large-scale pre-registered replications existed. Competing labs had begun publishing null incremental validity results. Claim was specifically 'better than IQ or conscientiousness' — a strong comparative statement requiring cross-domain generalization.
known_outcome: Credé et al. 2017 meta-analysis (88 independent samples, n>66,000) found grit's incremental validity over conscientiousness was negligible (r=0.02 incremental over conscientiousness for academic performance). Consistency-of-interest subscale showed weak validity; perseverance-of-effort subscale largely redundant with conscientiousness. The 'better than IQ' claim was also refuted by large-scale studies. Scientific consensus: grit is a repackaging of conscientiousness with weak incremental validity.
ground_truth_notes: The generalization failure is the decisive issue: the 'any domain, better than IQ/conscientiousness' claim was never supported cross-domain in independent samples. The high overlap with conscientiousness meant the construct itself was questionable. By 2016 the warning signs (construct validity, small samples, no hostile replication) were visible to a careful evaluator.
sources: Duckworth et al. 2007, Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
Credé et al. 2017, Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
Muenks et al. 2017, Journal of Research in Personality
Duckworth & Gross 2014, Perspectives on Psychological Science

hypothesis: Implicit Association Test (IAT) scores can be reduced through bias awareness training, and reductions in IAT scores translate into reduced discriminatory behavior in real-world settings.
evidence_available: Greenwald et al. 1998 launched the IAT; by 2015 over 14 million tests taken online. Multiple lab studies showed IAT scores could be shifted via counter-stereotypic priming. Implicit-explicit attitude dissociation well documented. However: test-retest reliability of IAT was low (r=0.44). Meta-analyses by Greenwald et al. (2009) estimated predictive validity (r=0.15-0.25) but independent meta-analyses were lower. Oswald et al. 2013 found poor predictive validity for discriminatory behavior specifically. No RCT of training had demonstrated real-world behavior change. Hostile replication data showing IAT-behavior correlations were unstable across contexts.
known_outcome: Forscher et al. 2019 meta-analysis (499 studies, n>87,000) found that attitude change interventions reliably shifted IAT scores but had near-zero effect on behavior (r=0.03). Lai et al. 2016 (17-procedure competition) found no procedure produced durable IAT reductions lasting more than a few hours. The IAT's predictive validity for behavior was subsequently estimated at r~0.15 in best-case conditions. Oswald et al. 2015 concluded the IAT should not be used to diagnose individual bias or predict behavior.
ground_truth_notes: The hostileSurvival failure reflects that the core causal chain (training reduces IAT, IAT reduction reduces behavior) was already known to be unreliable in 2015 from Oswald et al.'s published critique. The hostile survival score of 0.28 reflects that any rigorous behavior-outcome RCT was likely to find near-null results, as Forscher et al. later confirmed.
sources: Greenwald et al. 1998, Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
Oswald et al. 2013, Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
Forscher et al. 2019, Psychological Bulletin
Lai et al. 2016, Journal of Experimental Psychology: General

hypothesis: Achieving world-class expertise in any domain requires approximately 10,000 hours of deliberate practice, and deliberate practice is the dominant predictor of expert performance.
evidence_available: Ericsson et al. 1993 (Psychological Review, n=30 violinists) estimated elite musicians had accumulated ~10,000 practice hours by age 20. Gladwell's 2008 'Outliers' popularized the '10,000-hour rule' as domain-general. Supporting evidence: chess (de Groot, Simon & Chase), music (Ericsson), sports coaching literature. However: original Ericsson paper was a single domain, small n, retrospective estimate. The 'any domain' claim required wide generalization. No controlled experiment exists (ethically impossible to randomize practice hours). Individual differences in starting age, innate ability, coaching quality were not controlled. The 'dominant predictor' claim was empirically untested across domains.
known_outcome: Macnamara et al. 2014 meta-analysis (88 studies) found deliberate practice explained 26% of variance in sports, 21% in music, 18% in education, and only 4% in professions — far from a 'dominant predictor'. Hambrick et al. 2014 showed that working memory capacity and starting age explained additional variance beyond practice. Gobet & Campitelli 2007 documented wide variation in practice hours among chess grandmasters (range 3,000-25,000). The '10,000 hour' figure was not a threshold that applied uniformly across domains.
ground_truth_notes: The generalization kill is decisive: the original study covered one domain (violin) at one institution, and the population claim ('any domain') was Gladwell's extrapolation. By 2012, chess and sport data already showed wide variation in hours-to-expertise. The confoundControl score of 0.22 reflects the absence of any design that controlled for initial talent or coaching quality, which are the most plausible confounds.
sources: Ericsson et al. 1993, Psychological Review
Gladwell 2008, Outliers (Little, Brown and Company)
Macnamara et al. 2014, Psychological Science
Hambrick et al. 2014, Intelligence
Gobet & Campitelli 2007, in Cambridge Handbook of Expertise and Expert Performance

hypothesis: Informing members of a stereotyped group of a negative stereotype about their group before a test significantly reduces their performance on that test compared to a non-threatened control group.
evidence_available: Steele & Aronson 1995 (JPSP) showed Black college students underperformed on verbal GRE items when race was made salient, but not in a control condition. By 2005: 200+ studies across gender (math), race (academics), age (memory), socioeconomic status. Multiple independent labs confirmed the effect. Different operationalizations (diagnostic framing, explicit mention of stereotype) all produced effects. Meta-analytic effects d=0.5-0.8. Mechanism proposed (working memory disruption via anxiety) had experimental support. Effect appeared across multiple countries and contexts.
known_outcome: Stereotype threat effects replicated robustly through 2010-2015 in high-quality pre-registered studies. Flore & Wicherts 2015 meta-analysis (gender-STEM, k=47) found d=0.22 after correcting for publication bias — smaller than original estimates but non-zero and significant. Subsequent large pre-registered replications (Finnigan & Corker 2016, Shewach et al. 2019 meta-analysis) confirmed the effect is real but effect sizes are smaller than initially reported (d=0.12-0.25 in high-quality samples). The phenomenon is genuine; magnitude was overstated in early work.
ground_truth_notes: By 2005 the breadth of replication across labs, populations, and domains was sufficient for GO. The subsequent literature refined effect size estimates downward but confirmed the existence of the phenomenon. The claimRequiresGeneralization is false because the hypothesis is framed as a general mechanism, not specific to one population — and the mechanism evidence was sufficient.
sources: Steele & Aronson 1995, Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
Nguyen & Ryan 2008, Psychological Bulletin (meta-analysis, k=116)
Flore & Wicherts 2015, Journal of Experimental Social Psychology
Spencer et al. 1999, Journal of Experimental Social Psychology

hypothesis: Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is more effective than other active therapies (pharmacotherapy, psychodynamic therapy, supportive counseling) for anxiety disorders.
evidence_available: By 2010: multiple large RCTs comparing CBT head-to-head with medication and other talk therapies across panic disorder, social anxiety, GAD, specific phobia, OCD, PTSD. Hofmann & Smits 2008 meta-analysis (k=27, n=1,496) showed CBT significantly better than control. Multiple RCTs showed CBT equal or superior to SSRIs in acute treatment and superior in relapse prevention. NICE guidelines recommended CBT as first-line. Multiple independent research groups (UK, US, Australia, Germany) confirmed. Dismantling studies clarified which components drove effects. Active comparator designs with attention controls conducted.
known_outcome: CBT superiority for anxiety has remained the scientific consensus through 2025. Cuijpers et al. meta-analyses (2019, 2021) confirmed CBT produces larger effects than waiting list and is comparable to or superior to medication. NICE and APA guidelines continue to list CBT as first-line. Subsequent research has refined which anxiety disorders show the largest CBT advantage (panic, phobia, social anxiety > GAD). The core superiority claim has been upheld.
ground_truth_notes: By 2010 the evidence base was large, multi-site, internationally replicated, and guideline-endorsed. The active comparator designs (not just waitlist controls) make confoundControl high. The effect has been remarkably stable across replication attempts spanning 30+ years.
sources: Hofmann & Smits 2008, Journal of Clinical Psychiatry
Cuijpers et al. 2019, Psychological Medicine
Norton & Price 2007, Journal of Nervous and Mental Disease (meta-analysis)
NICE Clinical Guidelines CG113 2011

hypothesis: Infant attachment style (secure vs. insecure, as measured by the Strange Situation) reliably predicts adult romantic relationship quality and functioning.
evidence_available: Ainsworth et al. 1978 established the Strange Situation classification. Hazan & Shaver 1987 proposed adult attachment styles paralleled infant patterns. By 2000: several longitudinal studies (Waters et al. 2000, Hamilton 2000) tracked attachment from infancy to adulthood. Prediction was modest (r=0.27 in Waters et al.). BUT: significant discontinuity — life events (parental divorce, abuse, therapy) changed attachment classification substantially. Self-report adult measures poorly correlated with AAI gold standard. Genetic factors not controlled. Stability from infancy to adulthood was lower than expected (kappa~0.35-0.41 across 20-year follow-ups).
known_outcome: Fraley 2002 meta-analysis found mean r=0.39 for stability of attachment from infancy to later development — meaningful but far from deterministic. Roisman et al. 2005 showed that early attachment predicted later relationship quality at modest levels when covariates were controlled. The current consensus (Fraley & Roisman 2019) is that early attachment has a real but small and highly conditional predictive relationship with adult outcomes. The strong 'reliably predicts' framing is not supported; the weaker 'modestly associated with' is.
ground_truth_notes: The evidence in 2000 was genuinely mixed: longitudinal data existed but the prediction was modest and subject to major intervening variable effects. The generalization score of 0.42 reflects that the Strange Situation in infancy is quite different from the complex social landscape of adult relationships. UNRESOLVED is correct: not enough evidence to KILL a real and partially replicated association, but not enough for GO given the modest and inconsistent predictive validity.
sources: Ainsworth et al. 1978, Patterns of Attachment (Lawrence Erlbaum)
Waters et al. 2000, Child Development
Fraley 2002, Developmental Psychology
Hazan & Shaver 1987, Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
Roisman et al. 2005, Developmental Psychology

hypothesis: Providing external rewards for an intrinsically motivated activity undermines subsequent intrinsic motivation for that activity (the overjustification effect), and intrinsic motivation dominates performance on complex tasks once basic material needs are met.
evidence_available: Deci 1971 (JPSP) first demonstrated that external rewards reduced intrinsic motivation in laboratory settings. Deci, Koestner & Ryan 1999 meta-analysis (128 studies, n=10,000+) confirmed the overjustification effect for tangible, expected, contingent rewards (d=−0.68). The effect was well-replicated across labs. Distinction between controlling rewards (undermine) and informational/autonomy-supporting rewards (do not undermine) theoretically and empirically supported. Self-determination theory provided mechanistic framework. Cameron & Pierce 1994 counter-meta-analysis contested results (methodological dispute resolved largely in Deci's favor by 1999).
known_outcome: The overjustification effect has remained robust through 2025. Cerasoli et al. 2014 meta-analysis (k=183, n=212,000) confirmed intrinsic motivation predicts performance quality, while extrinsic incentives predict quantity. The distinction between reward types (contingent-expected vs. unexpected/non-contingent) is well-validated. Murayama et al. 2010 neuroimaging study confirmed reduced activation in intrinsic motivation circuitry after external reward. The core effect is accepted, though boundary conditions are nuanced.
ground_truth_notes: By 2000, the 128-study meta-analysis represented an unusually large and consistent evidence base for a psychological effect. The theoretical grounding in self-determination theory and the resolution of the methodological dispute with Cameron & Pierce strengthened the verdict. The high hostile survival reflects that adversarial replications and competing meta-analyses had already been published and the effect survived.
sources: Deci 1971, Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
Deci, Koestner & Ryan 1999, Psychological Bulletin (meta-analysis, k=128)
Cameron & Pierce 1994, Review of Educational Research
Cerasoli et al. 2014, Psychological Bulletin
