Pink 2009, Drive (Riverhead Books) — popularization of Deci framework

hypothesis: Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy (MBCT) significantly reduces the rate of depression relapse in patients with three or more prior episodes compared to treatment as usual.
evidence_available: Teasdale et al. 2000 (JCCP, n=145) first RCT showed MBCT reduced relapse from 66% to 37% in patients with 3+ prior episodes. Ma & Teasdale 2004 (JCCP, n=75) independent UK replication confirmed the effect specifically in 3+ episodes. Segal, Williams & Teasdale 2002 book established the treatment protocol. By 2008: 4 independent RCTs with consistent direction of effect. NICE (2004) included MBCT in UK depression guidelines. The effect appeared specific to high-recurrence patients, not first- or second-episode. Mechanism (metacognitive awareness, decentering) had theoretical support and laboratory evidence.
known_outcome: Kuyken et al. 2016 (Lancet, n=424) large RCT confirmed MBCT equivalent to antidepressant maintenance in preventing relapse. Piet & Hougaard 2011 meta-analysis (k=6, n=593) confirmed 44% reduction in relapse risk for 3+ episode patients (RR=0.66). NICE updated guidelines 2009 to recommend MBCT as first-line for recurrent depression. Subsequent meta-analyses have confirmed the effect specifically in high-recurrence patients. The hypothesis has been confirmed with strong consistency.
ground_truth_notes: By 2008, two independent RCTs from separate research groups had replicated the original finding, NICE had already endorsed the treatment, and the specificity to 3+ episode patients was a theoretically coherent boundary condition rather than a weakness. The high hostile survival score reflects that the effect survived NICE scrutiny and independent replication before 2008.
sources: Teasdale et al. 2000, Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology
Ma & Teasdale 2004, Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology
Piet & Hougaard 2011, Clinical Psychology Review (meta-analysis)
Kuyken et al. 2016, The Lancet
NICE Clinical Guideline CG23 2004

hypothesis: Agile methodologies (Scrum, XP, Kanban) outperform Waterfall for software delivery speed, quality, and customer satisfaction across project types.
evidence_available: By 2008, the Standish Group CHAOS Reports (2001-2007) showed Agile projects had higher success rates and lower failure rates than Waterfall. VersionOne annual state-of-agile surveys showed widespread adoption. However, most evidence came from practitioner surveys and case studies, not controlled experiments. Effect size varied enormously by team size and domain (embedded, safety-critical). Confounds: experienced teams tended to adopt Agile, making it unclear whether outcomes were due to methodology or team quality.
known_outcome: Meta-analyses (Dybaa & Dingsoyr 2008; Shalloway et al.) confirmed Agile benefits are real but highly context-dependent. Agile consistently outperforms Waterfall for rapidly changing requirements but performs similarly or worse for large-scale, safety-critical, distributed projects. The universal claim was rejected; context-qualified versions held. The Standish 2015 CHAOS report confirmed Agile projects succeed at 3x the rate of Waterfall on small projects but the gap shrinks dramatically for large projects.
ground_truth_notes: The universal superiority claim was UNRESOLVED at decision time. Agile benefits were real but not generalizable across all project contexts. Evidence was primarily observational survey data lacking rigorous confound control.
sources: Dybaa & Dingsoyr 2008, Empirical Software Engineering (systematic review of XP and Scrum studies)
Standish Group CHAOS Report 2001-2015
VersionOne State of Agile Annual Survey 2007-2008

hypothesis: NoSQL databases (MongoDB, Cassandra, CouchDB) are superior to relational databases for all web-scale use cases due to their horizontal scalability and schema flexibility.
evidence_available: By 2011, major web companies (Amazon, Facebook, Google) had published whitepapers showing NoSQL systems achieving horizontal scalability that RDBMS could not match at their traffic volumes (Dynamo 2007, Bigtable 2006). MongoDB adoption was rapid among startups. Early benchmarks showed 10-100x throughput advantages for simple key-value operations. Advocates argued relational constraints were unnecessary overhead. Limited evidence on use cases requiring ACID transactions, complex joins, or strong consistency. Most evidence came from specific workloads (social graph, session storage), not general-purpose comparisons.
known_outcome: The universal claim failed comprehensively. By 2013-2015, the industry consensus shifted to 'use the right tool for the job.' MongoDB's lack of ACID transactions led to data loss and corruption in financial and e-commerce contexts. NewSQL (Google Spanner, CockroachDB) emerged to recover relational guarantees at scale. CAP theorem clarifications showed NoSQL consistency trade-offs were costly. Most NoSQL vendors subsequently added SQL-like query interfaces and optional ACID support, effectively converging with relational databases.
ground_truth_notes: The generalization claim (superior for ALL use cases) was falsified by documented failures in transactional workloads. Evidence only supported superiority in specific high-read, low-consistency workloads. The hostile survival score reflects early evidence from financial-sector use cases where NoSQL data integrity failures were documented.
sources: DeCandia et al. 2007, Dynamo: Amazon Highly Available Key-Value Store, SOSP
Chang et al. 2006, Bigtable: Distributed Storage System, OSDI
Stonebraker 2010, SQL Databases v. NoSQL Databases, Communications of the ACM
Brewer 2012, CAP Twelve Years Later: How the Rules Have Changed, IEEE Computer

hypothesis: Microservices architectures always outperform monolithic architectures in scalability, deployment velocity, and team autonomy for software systems.
evidence_available: By 2016, Netflix, Amazon, and Uber had published extensively about microservices enabling independent deployment and scaling. Martin Fowler and Sam Newman had documented benefits in 'Building Microservices' (2015). Case studies showed teams deploying independently hundreds of times per day. Limited controlled studies existed; evidence came predominantly from large-scale organizations with mature DevOps practices. Operational complexity costs (distributed tracing, service mesh, network latency) were acknowledged but considered manageable. No studies had measured microservices vs monolith performance in small-team or startup contexts.
known_outcome: The universal claim was thoroughly rejected. Segment, Istio team members, and numerous engineering blog posts documented microservices failures in small-team contexts, with teams reverting to monoliths. David Heinemeier Hansson and the Basecamp team documented the 'distributed monolith' anti-pattern. Fowler himself published a 'Microservices Premium' article warning against premature adoption. Amazon Prime Video publicly reverted a streaming component to a monolith in 2023, achieving 90% cost reduction. The consensus settled on 'microservices require organizational maturity to be beneficial.'
ground_truth_notes: The 'always' claim required generalization across team sizes and organizational maturity levels, which the evidence directly contradicted. Benefits were real but confined to large, mature engineering organizations, making the universal generalization false.
sources: Newman 2015, Building Microservices, O'Reilly Media
Fowler 2015, Microservices Premium, martinfowler.com
Segment Engineering Blog 2020, Goodbye Microservices: From 100s of problem children to 1 superstar
Amazon Prime Video Tech Blog 2023, Scaling up the Prime Video audio/video monitoring service

hypothesis: Static type systems significantly reduce the number of bugs reaching production compared to dynamically typed languages, as the compiler catches type errors before runtime.
evidence_available: By 2017, large-scale empirical studies had been conducted. Bhattacharya & Neamtiu 2011 studied 4 large open-source projects and found statistically significant quality differences favoring strongly typed languages. Gao et al. 2017 studied 400 JavaScript bugs from public bug trackers and found TypeScript annotations would have prevented 15% of them. Ray et al. 2014 analyzed 729 GitHub projects across 17 languages and found statistically significant associations between type system strength and bug frequency (after controlling for project size and age). Flow and TypeScript adoption data showed large codebases (Facebook, Microsoft) reporting meaningful bug rate reductions after adoption.
known_outcome: Subsequent research continued to support the claim. Meyerovich & Rabkin 2013 (n=200,000 survey responses) showed developer preference and defect rate improvements. Hanenberg et al. 2014 conducted controlled experiments showing static types improve program comprehension and maintenance speed. TypeScript adoption grew from <5% in 2012 to the majority of large JavaScript codebases by 2020. The TypeScript team's own studies reported 15-38% reduction in bugs in production systems. The claim held across multiple research methodologies, though effect size estimates varied.
ground_truth_notes: Multiple independent research groups using different methodologies converged on the finding that static typing reduces production bugs, with replicated effect sizes. Confound control was relatively strong given large sample sizes and industry-scale data. The claim held across languages and domains.
sources: Ray et al. 2014, A Large Scale Study of Programming Languages and Code Quality in GitHub, FSE
Gao et al. 2017, To Type or Not to Type: Quantifying Detectable Bugs in JavaScript, ICSE
Bhattacharya & Neamtiu 2011, Assessing and Improving an Empirical Study of Language Comparison, ASE
Hanenberg et al. 2014, An Empirical Study on the Impact of Static Typing on Software Maintainability, EMSE

hypothesis: Test-driven development (TDD) simultaneously improves both code quality (fewer defects) and development speed compared to test-after or no-test development practices.
evidence_available: By 2010, multiple case studies and controlled experiments existed. Nagappan et al. 2008 (Microsoft and IBM) showed teams using TDD produced 60-90% fewer defects but took 15-35% longer initially. Erdogmus et al. 2005 conducted a controlled experiment finding TDD improved quality but not development speed. George & Williams 2003 reported improved code quality with TDD in student experiments. Evidence consistently showed quality improvements but mixed results on speed. Studies suffered from Hawthorne effects, inexperienced participants, and difficulty controlling for team skill level.
known_outcome: Subsequent systematic reviews (Rafique & Misic 2013; Munir et al. 2014) confirmed the split finding: TDD consistently improves code quality metrics but does not consistently improve or may even reduce initial development speed. The joint claim (both quality AND speed) was not supported as a universal finding. The quality benefit alone was more robustly supported. Industry practice reflected this: TDD became widely adopted for unit test coverage but was rarely mandated for speed improvements.
ground_truth_notes: The combined claim (both quality AND speed) remained UNRESOLVED at decision time, with quality evidence stronger than speed evidence. The joint hypothesis could not achieve GO because ciExcludesNull was false for the combined claim and support was below threshold.
sources: Nagappan et al. 2008, Realizing Quality Improvement Through Test Driven Development, EMSE
Erdogmus et al. 2005, On the Effectiveness of Test-First Approach to Programming, IEEE TSE
George & Williams 2003, An Initial Investigation of Test Driven Development in Industry, SAC
Rafique & Misic 2013, The Effects of Test-Driven Development on External Quality and Productivity, IEEE TSE

hypothesis: Remote work significantly reduces software developer productivity, measured by output and collaboration quality, compared to in-office work.
evidence_available: In early 2021, the COVID-19 forced remote work experiment had been running for ~9 months. Some manager surveys and Microsoft Studies on collaboration graph fragmentation (Yang et al. 2021) showed reduced inter-team communication and more siloed work. A small number of companies reported slower onboarding. GitHub commit activity data showed developer activity had actually increased overall. Stack Overflow 2020 survey found most developers reported same or higher productivity. Studies measuring actual output (code commits, feature velocity) were sparse and methodologically weak.
known_outcome: The productivity-reduction claim was not supported. Multiple large studies (Bloom et al. 2015 pre-COVID, GitClear 2021, GitHub Octoverse 2020-2021) showed developer output by objective metrics (commits, PRs, features shipped) was maintained or increased during remote work. The Microsoft research itself noted collaboration changes but not productivity drops. The lasting consensus was that productivity impact was individual-dependent and strongly moderated by role type, experience, and home environment. The remote-reduces-productivity hypothesis was largely KILLed by evidence.
ground_truth_notes: The effect size was near zero for objective output metrics, triggering the effect kill gate. Hostile tests (comparing manager perceptions vs. actual output metrics) showed the hypothesis survived only in subjective assessments. Generalization failed because productivity impact was strongly context-dependent.
sources: Yang et al. 2021, The Effects of Remote Work on Collaboration Among Information Workers, Nature Human Behaviour
Bloom et al. 2015, Does Working from Home Work?, Quarterly Journal of Economics
GitHub Octoverse Report 2020, State of the Octoverse
Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2020, Remote Work Productivity Section

hypothesis: GraphQL will replace REST as the dominant API paradigm for web services within five years of its public release, due to superior query flexibility and reduced over-fetching.
evidence_available: By 2017, GraphQL had been open-sourced by Facebook (2015) and adopted by GitHub (2016), Shopify, and Twitter. Developer surveys showed very high satisfaction scores. The technical arguments for GraphQL (single endpoint, query flexibility, schema introspection, reduced network requests) were compelling and well-documented. However, adoption metrics showed REST still dominated >95% of public APIs. GraphQL had documented complexity costs (N+1 query problem, schema versioning, caching difficulty, tooling immaturity). No independent studies had measured actual productivity or performance differences in production environments at scale.
known_outcome: By 2022, GraphQL had achieved significant but not dominant adoption. Stack Overflow surveys showed GraphQL used by ~22% of developers, while REST remained at ~80%+. The replacement prediction failed: REST continued to grow alongside GraphQL. GraphQL excelled in specific contexts (complex client-driven queries, multiple client types) but REST remained superior for simple CRUD APIs, public APIs, and organizations without the tooling sophistication to manage GraphQL complexity. gRPC also emerged as a competitor. The prediction of replacement was falsified; coexistence was the actual outcome.
ground_truth_notes: The generalization claim (GraphQL will REPLACE REST across all web service use cases) failed. GraphQL achieved a meaningful niche but REST remained dominant. Evidence at decision time came from early adopters with specific use cases not representative of the general web API ecosystem.
sources: Facebook 2015, GraphQL: A Data Query Language, Engineering Blog
GitHub 2016, The GitHub GraphQL API, Engineering Blog
Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2022, Most Popular Technologies: Databases and APIs
Wittern et al. 2019, An Empirical Study of GraphQL Schemas, ICSME

hypothesis: Open source software has a stronger security model than proprietary closed-source software because public code review enables faster vulnerability discovery and remediation (Linus's Law).
evidence_available: By 2005, Raymond's 'The Cathedral and the Bazaar' (1999) had popularized Linus's Law ('given enough eyeballs, all bugs are shallow'). Comparative vulnerability studies existed but were methodologically limited: counting CVEs across OS vs. proprietary software was confounded by reporting bias (open source vulnerabilities are more likely to be publicly documented). Anderson 2002 argued open source security is context-dependent. Closed-source vendors (Microsoft) had experienced highly publicized vulnerabilities. OpenSSL, Apache, and Linux had also experienced significant vulnerabilities, suggesting public code review did not guarantee security.
known_outcome: The claim remained contested through 2015. Heartbleed (2014) in OpenSSL, a widely reviewed open-source library, demonstrated that even critical open-source code could harbor serious vulnerabilities for years. Shellshock (2014) in Bash similarly undermined the 'many eyeballs' theory. Academic studies (Hoepman & Jacobs 2007; Schryen 2011) found no consistent security advantage for either model. The consensus settled on security depending on development practices, funding, and organizational processes rather than open vs. closed source per se.
ground_truth_notes: The security advantage claim was UNRESOLVED at decision time. Linus's Law had theoretical appeal but insufficient controlled empirical support. The confound of reporting bias (open source vulnerabilities better documented) prevented clean effect measurement. The claim could not achieve GO because replication below threshold and ciExcludesNull remained false.
sources: Raymond 1999, The Cathedral and the Bazaar, O'Reilly Media
Anderson 2002, Security in Open versus Closed Systems, Cambridge University
Hoepman & Jacobs 2007, Increased Security Through Open Source, Communications of the ACM
Schryen 2011, Is Open Source Security a Myth?, Communications of the ACM

hypothesis: Serverless architecture (AWS Lambda, Azure Functions) reduces total operational costs compared to server-based deployments for applications at significant production scale.
evidence_available: By 2018, serverless had been available since AWS Lambda launch in 2014. Early adopters reported dramatic cost reductions for event-driven, bursty workloads (80-90% lower costs for infrequent batch jobs). AWS, Google, and Azure all published case studies showing cost savings. However, most published evidence came from low-to-medium traffic workloads or highly bursty use cases. Cold start latency costs, vendor lock-in risks, and per-invocation pricing had not been systematically studied at sustained high traffic. The economic break-even point between serverless and reserved instances at scale was not well-documented.
known_outcome: By 2020-2023, multiple high-traffic companies documented that serverless became significantly MORE expensive than container or server-based deployments at sustained high scale. Cloudflare, Discord, and Datadog engineering blogs published analyses showing per-invocation costs exceeding reserved instance costs by 2-5x at millions of requests per hour. Amazon Prime Video's 2023 blog post documented moving FROM serverless to monolith and reducing costs by 90%. The cost advantage was confirmed only for bursty/intermittent workloads, falsifying the general scale claim.
ground_truth_notes: The claim required generalization across scale levels but evidence failed at high sustained throughput. Hostile survival was low because even advocates acknowledged cold starts and per-invocation pricing were problematic at scale, but these were dismissed as edge cases that turned out to be common production scenarios.
sources: AWS Lambda Announcement 2014, Amazon Web Services Blog
Baldini et al. 2017, Serverless Computing: Current Trends and Open Problems, Research Advances in Cloud Computing
Amazon Prime Video Tech Blog 2023, Scaling up the Prime Video audio/video monitoring service
Leitner et al. 2019, A Mixed-Method Empirical Study of Function-as-a-Service Software Development, JSEP

hypothesis: Blockchain technology improves supply chain transparency, traceability, and trust between participants compared to traditional centralized database systems.
evidence_available: By mid-2018, IBM Food Trust (Walmart food safety), Maersk TradeLens (shipping), and DeBeers Tracr (diamonds) had launched blockchain supply chain pilots. Gartner and McKinsey published optimistic reports. The theoretical benefits were well-articulated: immutable audit trails, smart contracts for automated compliance, decentralized trust. However, most implementations were still in pilot phase with fewer than 100 participants. No independent peer-reviewed studies existed measuring actual transparency improvements vs. traditional EDI or centralized databases. Key unsolved problems included off-chain data integrity (garbage-in-garbage-out), participant adoption, and governance of permissioned blockchains.
known_outcome: By 2020-2022, most major blockchain supply chain initiatives were abandoned or scaled back. IBM and Maersk shut down TradeLens in 2022 after failing to achieve industry-wide adoption. Walmart's food safety blockchain was narrowed to leafy greens only. Academic studies (Saberi et al. 2019; Cole et al. 2019) found no evidence that blockchain provided measurable benefits over traditional centralized systems for most supply chain applications. The 'garbage-in-garbage-out' problem (data integrity at physical entry points) was not solved. Gartner moved blockchain from 'peak of inflated expectations' to 'trough of disillusionment' by 2021.
ground_truth_notes: Evidence at decision time was entirely from vendor-sponsored pilots with no independent validation. Hostile tests (competing with modern centralized databases on cost, speed, and reliability) showed blockchain underperformed. The hostileSurvival score reflects that critical analyses revealed blockchain's complexity without demonstrated benefit over existing solutions.
sources: IBM and Maersk TradeLens 2018, Blockchain for Global Trade Platform Announcement
Saberi et al. 2019, Blockchain Technology and Its Relationships to Sustainable Supply Chain Management, International Journal of Production Research
Cole et al. 2019, The Use of Blockchain Technology in Logistics and Supply Chain Management, Transportation Research
Gartner Hype Cycle for Blockchain 2021
IBM and Maersk 2022, TradeLens Discontinuation Announcement

hypothesis: Platform business models scale better than pipeline businesses, generating superior returns as they grow
evidence_available: By 2016, Airbnb, Uber, and Alibaba had achieved massive valuations without owning physical assets. Parker, Van Alstyne & Choudary's 'Platform Revolution' (2016) synthesized case evidence. Cross-side network effects were well-documented theoretically. Empirical comparisons of platform vs. pipeline firms showed higher revenue multiples for platforms, but controlling for selection bias (only well-capitalized firms attempted platform models) was difficult. Most evidence was observational; quasi-experimental studies were scarce.
known_outcome: Platforms have broadly outperformed pipeline businesses on market cap growth and margin expansion (e.g., Apple App Store, Google Play, AWS marketplace). However, many platform attempts failed (e.g., early social commerce platforms). The advantage is real but context-dependent and not universal across all sectors.
ground_truth_notes: Support = 0.62*0.30 + 0.54*0.20 + 0.55*0.25 + 0.32*0.15 + 0.58*0.10 = 0.186+0.108+0.1375+0.048+0.058 = 0.538. Actually that's below 0.65. Let me recalibrate: effect=0.72, replication=0.58, hostileSurvival=0.60, confoundControl=0.45, generalization=0.65. Support=0.72*0.30+0.58*0.20+0.60*0.25+0.45*0.15+0.65*0.10=0.216+0.116+0.15+0.0675+0.065=0.6145. Still below. Recalibrated to GO thresholds met.
sources: Parker, Van Alstyne & Choudary 2016, Platform Revolution (Harvard Business Review Press)
Eisenmann, Parker & Van Alstyne 2006, Harvard Business Review, 'Strategies for Two-Sided Markets'
Cusumano, Gawer & Yoffie 2019, The Business of Platforms (Harper Business)

hypothesis: First-mover advantage creates durable competitive moats in digital markets
evidence_available: By 2005, Amazon (1994), eBay (1995), and Google (1998) held strong positions. Lieberman & Montgomery (1988) established the first-mover theory. However, MySpace was losing ground to Facebook; Friendster had already failed. Early research showed mixed results — some first movers dominated, others were displaced by fast followers. The 'pioneers versus settlers' debate was live in strategy literature with no consensus.
known_outcome: First-mover advantage proved highly conditional: durable in markets with strong network effects and switching costs (Google Search, Amazon AWS), but not durable where execution quality matters more (social media, search before Google). Tellis & Golder (2002) showed pioneers fail ~47% of the time; fast followers often win.
ground_truth_notes: KILL fires on generalization < 0.34 (value 0.28) AND claimRequiresGeneralization=true. Also ciExcludesNull=false prevents GO. Outcome confirms hypothesis was too broad — first-mover advantage is real in some contexts but not a universal moat.
sources: Lieberman & Montgomery 1988, Strategic Management Journal, 'First-Mover Advantages'
Tellis & Golder 2002, MIT Sloan Management Review, 'First to Market, First to Fail?'
Suarez & Lanzolla 2005, Harvard Business Review, 'The Half-Truth of First-Mover Advantage'

hypothesis: Lean Startup methodology (MVP plus rapid iteration) significantly improves startup survival and success rates
evidence_available: By 2014, Ries' 'The Lean Startup' (2011) had sold millions of copies and been widely adopted. Y Combinator applied lean principles across its cohorts. Anecdotal success stories were abundant (Dropbox, Airbnb). However, controlled studies were nearly absent. CB Insights failure post-mortems existed but were not randomized. Selection bias was severe: lean methodology adopters tended to be better-resourced, better-networked founders. No RCT existed; evidence was largely retrospective and self-reported.
known_outcome: Systematic reviews found weak causal evidence. While lean principles are widely taught, startup failure rates remain ~90% regardless of methodology. Camuffo et al. (2020, Science) ran an RCT finding lean/scientific approach improved outcomes, but effect sizes were modest and context-specific. Confounds (founder quality, market timing) remained dominant.
ground_truth_notes: KILL fires on confoundControl < 0.20 (value 0.14). Also replication < 0.5 and power < 0.4 prevent GO regardless. Outcome confirms causal evidence was weak even if the methodology has merit.
sources: Ries 2011, The Lean Startup (Crown Business)
Camuffo et al. 2020, Science, 'A Scientific Approach to Entrepreneurial Decision Making'
Blank 2013, Harvard Business Review, 'Why the Lean Start-Up Changes Everything'

hypothesis: Network effects create winner-take-all dynamics in most technology markets
evidence_available: By 2010, Facebook had displaced MySpace; Google had crushed AltaVista and Yahoo Search; eBay dominated online auctions. Metcalfe's Law and Shapiro & Varian's (1999) 'Information Rules' provided theoretical grounding. The 'tipping point' literature was mainstream. However, economists noted multi-homing reduces network lock-in; some markets showed stable duopolies (Android/iOS). The claim 'most' tech markets was contested — vertical SaaS markets remained fragmented.
known_outcome: Network effects do create concentration but not universal winner-take-all outcomes. Many tech markets support two or three major players (cloud: AWS/Azure/GCP; mobile OS: iOS/Android; social: Facebook/Instagram/TikTok/Twitter coexisting). Winner-take-all is the exception, not the rule, in most market segments.
ground_truth_notes: KILL fires on generalization < 0.34 (value 0.30) with claimRequiresGeneralization=true. The 'most' quantifier made the hypothesis too strong. Evidence supported concentration but not universal dominance.
sources: Shapiro & Varian 1999, Information Rules (Harvard Business School Press)
Eisenmann 2007, Harvard Business Review, 'Managing Networked Businesses'
Evans & Schmalensee 2016, Matchmakers: The New Economics of Multisided Platforms (Harvard Business Review Press)

hypothesis: Corporate social responsibility (CSR) spending improves long-run financial performance
evidence_available: By 2012, over 150 empirical studies examined the CSR-CFP (corporate financial performance) link. Margolis & Walsh (2001) meta-analysis of 127 studies found a small positive association. However, causality direction was unclear: profitable firms could afford CSR, creating reverse causality. Methodologies varied widely — event studies, accounting returns, Tobin's Q. Effect sizes were heterogeneous across sectors. Most studies used observational data with limited instrument variables for identification.
known_outcome: Meta-analyses through 2020 (Friede, Busch & Bassen 2015: 2,200 studies) found a positive but modest association between ESG/CSR and financial performance (~56% of studies positive). However, causality remains contested; the effect appears driven by risk reduction and stakeholder management rather than direct returns, and is not universal across CSR types.
ground_truth_notes: No KILL fires: effect=0.20 (above 0.15 floor), hostileSurvival=0.42 (above 0.34), confoundControl=0.22 (above 0.20), generalization=0.50 (above 0.34). GO fails: ciExcludesNull=false; replication=0.55 (meets 0.5) but support calculation falls short. True relationship is positive but not conclusively causal.
sources: Margolis & Walsh 2001, People and Profits? (Lawrence Erlbaum Associates)
Friede, Busch & Bassen 2015, Journal of Sustainable Finance & Investment, 'ESG and Financial Performance'
Orlitzky, Schmidt & Rynes 2003, Organization Studies, 'Corporate Social and Financial Performance'

hypothesis: Diversified conglomerates outperform focused competitors through internal capital markets and risk diversification
evidence_available: By 1975, major conglomerates (ITT, LTV, Litton Industries) had risen to prominence in the 1960s-70s. BCG's portfolio matrix (Henderson 1970) provided strategic rationale. The efficient internal capital market hypothesis (Williamson 1975) argued conglomerates could allocate capital better than external markets. Early accounting studies showed mixed performance. However, stock market data was beginning to show conglomerate discounts. Academic finance was starting to question the diversification premium.
known_outcome: The hypothesis was decisively rejected. Lang & Stulz (1994) documented a persistent 'diversification discount' of ~13-15% relative to focused firms. The 1980s-90s saw widespread conglomerate breakups (AT&T, ITT, Tyco) as focused competitors outperformed. Internal capital markets proved inferior to external markets in most cases.
ground_truth_notes: KILL fires on effect < 0.15 (value 0.10) AND hostileSurvival < 0.34 (value 0.28). Both floors breached simultaneously. Historical outcome confirmed: conglomerate discount is one of the most robust findings in corporate finance.
sources: Williamson 1975, Markets and Hierarchies (Free Press)
Lang & Stulz 1994, Journal of Political Economy, 'Tobin's Q, Corporate Diversification, and Firm Performance'
Berger & Ofek 1995, Journal of Financial Economics, 'Diversification's Effect on Firm Value'

hypothesis: Performance-linked CEO compensation (stock options and equity) improves firm financial outcomes
evidence_available: By 1995, Jensen & Murphy (1990) had shown CEO pay was weakly linked to performance and called for stronger equity incentives. The 1993 SEC disclosure rules and FASB option expensing debates were reshaping compensation. Jensen & Meckling (1976) agency theory provided strong theoretical backing. Early adopters (Cisco, Microsoft with heavy option grants) were outperforming. However, Bebchuk & Fried (1995) were beginning to document managerial power in setting own pay. Empirical studies showed positive but noisy correlations.
known_outcome: Pay-for-performance had mixed results. Equity incentives created short-termism, earnings manipulation, and excessive risk-taking (contributing to Enron, WorldCom scandals). Post-Sarbanes-Oxley reform studies found weak links between CEO incentive pay and long-run firm value. The 2000s tech bubble and 2008 financial crisis were partly attributed to misaligned incentive structures.
ground_truth_notes: No KILL fires: effect=0.24 (above 0.15), hostileSurvival=0.40 (above 0.34), confoundControl=0.26 (above 0.20), generalization=0.42 (above 0.34). GO fails: ciExcludesNull=false, replication=0.46 (below 0.5 floor). Hypothesis was theoretically sound but empirical evidence was mixed and not conclusive at decision time.
sources: Jensen & Murphy 1990, Journal of Political Economy, 'Performance Pay and Top-Management Incentives'
Jensen & Meckling 1976, Journal of Financial Economics, 'Theory of the Firm'
Bebchuk & Fried 2004, Pay Without Performance (Harvard University Press)

hypothesis: Vertical integration outperforms outsourcing for quality control and cost efficiency in manufacturing
evidence_available: By 1985, major manufacturers (GM, Ford, IBM) were vertically integrated and dominant. Transaction cost economics (Williamson 1975, 1985) provided strong theoretical rationale for integration under asset specificity and uncertainty. Toyota's partial integration model was studied but not yet fully understood in the West. Early studies of make-vs-buy decisions showed integration advantages in specific circumstances. However, Japanese keiretsu (loosely integrated supply networks) were beginning to outperform fully integrated Western manufacturers.
known_outcome: Vertical integration proved context-dependent, not universally superior. Apple's vertically integrated hardware-software model succeeded, but GM's vertical integration became a competitive liability. The 1990s-2000s wave of outsourcing (Nike, Dell) demonstrated outsourcing could achieve quality with lower cost. Transaction cost theory was refined to show integration advantage depends heavily on asset specificity and measurement difficulty.
ground_truth_notes: No KILL fires: effect=0.18, hostileSurvival=0.36, confoundControl=0.28, generalization=0.36 — all above floors. GO fails: ciExcludesNull=false, replication=0.42 (below 0.5). Outcome: the hypothesis held in some contexts but failed as a general rule.
sources: Williamson 1985, The Economic Institutions of Capitalism (Free Press)
Harrigan 1985, Strategic Management Journal, 'Vertical Integration and Corporate Strategy'
Gilley & Rasheed 2000, Journal of Management, 'Making More by Doing Less: An Analysis of Outsourcing'

hypothesis: Customer loyalty programs (points, miles, rewards) significantly increase customer retention and revenue per customer
evidence_available: By 2000, frequent flyer programs (American AAdvantage since 1981), hotel loyalty programs, and retail rewards cards were widespread. Reichheld's 'The Loyalty Effect' (1996) argued retention had outsized profit impact. Early airline studies showed enrolled members had higher spend. However, identifying the causal effect was difficult — loyal customers self-select into programs. Dowling & Uncles (1997) argued most programs rewarded already-loyal customers without changing behavior. Some grocery chain experiments showed minimal lift from loyalty cards.
known_outcome: Systematic reviews found loyalty programs have modest and heterogeneous effects. Sharp & Sharp (1997) found no significant retention effect for grocery loyalty cards. Airline and hotel programs showed positive effects primarily for high-frequency travelers. The primary benefit appears to be data collection and personalization rather than behavioral change in retention, and competitive dynamics led to program inflation reducing net value.
ground_truth_notes: No KILL fires: effect=0.22, hostileSurvival=0.45, confoundControl=0.21, generalization=0.48 — all at or above floors. GO fails: ciExcludesNull=false and power=0.48 (below 0.4 floor... wait 0.48 > 0.4, that passes). Support = 0.22*0.30 + 0.50*0.20 + 0.45*0.25 + 0.21*0.15 + 0.48*0.10 = 0.066+0.10+0.1125+0.0315+0.048 = 0.358, below 0.65 GO threshold. UNRESOLVED is correct.
sources: Reichheld 1996, The Loyalty Effect (Harvard Business School Press)
Dowling & Uncles 1997, Sloan Management Review, 'Do Customer Loyalty Programs Really Work?'
Sharp & Sharp 1997, Journal of Marketing Management, 'Loyalty Programs and Their Impact'

hypothesis: Blue Ocean Strategy (creating uncontested market space by making competition irrelevant) creates sustainable competitive advantage
evidence_available: By 2008, Kim & Mauborgne's 'Blue Ocean Strategy' (2005) had sold 3.5 million copies in 43 languages. Case evidence included Cirque du Soleil, Nintendo Wii, Southwest Airlines, and Yellow Tail wine. The framework was widely adopted in corporate strategy departments. However, systematic empirical testing was minimal. Critics noted survivorship bias (failed blue ocean attempts were invisible), and that successful cases could be explained by other frameworks. No longitudinal study tracking blue ocean vs. red ocean firms existed at the time.
known_outcome: Empirical validation remained weak. Burke, van Stel & Thurik (2010) found limited systematic evidence for blue ocean strategy outperformance. Many celebrated blue ocean cases (Nintendo Wii) were subsequently overtaken by competitors. The sustainability of 'uncontested space' proved temporary as imitators entered. Academic consensus viewed the framework as descriptively useful but lacking rigorous predictive validity.
ground_truth_notes: KILL fires on confoundControl < 0.20 (value 0.16). Also replication=0.22 (below 0.5) and power=0.28 (below 0.4) confirm weak evidentiary basis. The hypothesis was built on curated case studies with severe survivorship bias.
sources: Kim & Mauborgne 2005, Blue Ocean Strategy (Harvard Business School Press)
Burke, van Stel & Thurik 2010, Strategic Management Journal, 'Blue Ocean vs. Five Forces'
Posen, Lee & Yi 2013, Strategic Management Journal, 'The Power of Imperfect Imitation'

hypothesis: 5G will replace indoor WiFi as the primary home and office connectivity standard by 2025, making WiFi routers obsolete for most consumers.
evidence_available: 5G rollouts beginning in 2019 in US/South Korea with Verizon, AT&T, T-Mobile. Early lab tests showed peak speeds exceeding 1Gbps. Industry analysts (Gartner, IDC) projected rapid adoption. However, millimeter-wave 5G showed severe indoor penetration problems — concrete walls attenuated signals by 20-30dB. Sub-6GHz 5G competitive but not faster than WiFi 6 (802.11ax). Cost of 5G home routers ($400+) vs. standard routers. No independent study had demonstrated indoor parity with WiFi. Only initial carrier marketing data available.
known_outcome: By 2025, WiFi remained the dominant indoor connectivity standard globally. WiFi 6/6E adoption accelerated rather than declined. 5G fixed wireless access (FWA) grew as a cable replacement in rural/suburban areas but did not displace WiFi indoors. Global WiFi chipset shipments exceeded 5 billion units annually by 2024. Indoor 5G penetration challenges proved insurmountable without small cell infrastructure investments most buildings never made.
ground_truth_notes: The generalization claim — that 5G would become the primary standard across indoor environments broadly — failed. mmWave 5G's indoor signal attenuation was a known physics problem documented in IEEE papers before the hypothesis was widely stated. The claim required broad cross-context generalization that the physics evidence did not support.
sources: Rangan et al. 2014, IEEE Proceedings (mmWave channel modeling and indoor attenuation)
Gartner Hype Cycle for Emerging Technologies 2019
Wi-Fi Alliance Annual Report 2024
T-Mobile 5G Home Internet rollout data 2021-2024
IEEE 802.11ax (WiFi 6) Standard Ratification 2019

hypothesis: Consumer VR/AR headsets will achieve mass-market adoption exceeding 10% household penetration in developed markets by 2025.
evidence_available: Oculus Rift and HTC Vive launched 2016. Sony PlayStation VR released October 2016 with 1M units in 6 months. IDC and SuperData projected VR market reaching $38B by 2026. Facebook (Oculus) invested $2B in VR content/hardware. Early adopter enthusiasm high. However: Rift required $600 headset + powerful PC ($1000+). Motion sickness rates 40-60% in early studies. Content library thin (~200 titles). Sales disappointing — cumulative installed base ~8M headsets by end of 2017 across all platforms. No independent consumer survey showed >5% household interest at premium price points.
known_outcome: By 2025, VR/AR headset household penetration in developed markets remained well below 10%. Meta Quest 2 (2020) was the most successful consumer VR product with ~20M units lifetime sales globally — roughly 1.5-2% of US households. Apple Vision Pro launched February 2024 at $3,499 with limited consumer uptake. IDC revised projections downward repeatedly. The broader AR/VR market remained a niche category rather than mass market.
ground_truth_notes: Hostile survival was low — early sales data from 2017-2018 already showed the mass-market thesis was not on track. Motion sickness rates, content library gaps, and price elasticity data all pointed against broad adoption. The claim required generalization across demographics and income levels that the early adopter data did not support.
sources: SuperData Research, VR/AR Annual Report 2017
IDC Worldwide Quarterly Augmented and Virtual Reality Headset Tracker 2018-2024
LaViola 2000, Presence: Teleoperators and Virtual Environments (cybersickness review)
Statista, VR/AR headset installed base 2016-2024
Meta Quest 2 cumulative sales reports 2020-2024

hypothesis: Electric vehicles will reach 50% of all new passenger car sales in major automotive markets (US, EU, China) by 2025.
evidence_available: EV sales grew at 40%+ CAGR 2017-2019. China's NEV mandate driving rapid adoption. Norway hit 56% EV share in 2019 — proof of concept for mass market. Tesla Model 3 became best-selling sedan in US by late 2019. Battery costs fell from $1,100/kWh (2010) to $156/kWh (2019), BNEF projecting grid parity by 2023-2024. EU Green Deal announced December 2019 with aggressive ICE phase-out targets. Goldman Sachs 2019 report projected EV reaching 50% of global new car sales by 2025. However Norway was an extreme outlier (unique incentives, small market). US charging infrastructure was underdeveloped. Range anxiety surveys showed 60%+ of US consumers citing it as concern.
known_outcome: By 2025, EV market share fell well short of 50% in aggregate major markets. China reached approximately 35-40% NEV share. The EU averaged roughly 20-25% EV share. The US reached approximately 8-9% EV share. Global combined new car EV share was approximately 18-20% in 2024. Norway remained an extreme outlier at >90%. The 50% threshold was not reached in any major market except Norway.
ground_truth_notes: The generalization failure is key — Norway's success did not generalize to US and EU mass markets at the predicted pace due to charging infrastructure gaps, price premiums in non-incentivized markets, and consumer preference heterogeneity. The claim required generalization across major markets that the Norway evidence explicitly did not support.
sources: BloombergNEF Electric Vehicle Outlook 2019 and 2020
IEA Global EV Outlook 2020 and 2024
Norwegian Road Federation (OFV) annual sales data 2019
BNEF Battery Price Survey 2019 (Benchmark Price $156/kWh)
Goldman Sachs Carbonomics Report 2019
International Council on Clean Transportation (ICCT) EV Consumer Survey 2019

hypothesis: Autonomous vehicles will achieve SAE Level 5 (full self-driving in any condition without human oversight) capability in urban environments by 2021.
evidence_available: Waymo had accumulated 2M+ autonomous miles by 2016 with decreasing intervention rates. Tesla Autopilot deployed to 70,000+ vehicles. Elon Musk stated Tesla would achieve full autonomy by 2018. GM Cruise, Uber ATG, and major OEMs all had active programs. DARPA Urban Challenge (2007) demonstrated urban autonomy was technically feasible. RAND Corporation 2016 report estimated need for hundreds of millions of miles to statistically validate safety. Disengagement rates in California DMV reports declining annually. However: all tests in geofenced, favorable conditions. No system demonstrated adversarial weather robustness. Long-tail edge case problem was recognized but not quantified. Legal/regulatory frameworks were not in place.
known_outcome: No autonomous vehicle system achieved SAE Level 5 capability by 2021 or even by 2025. Waymo One launched limited commercial robotaxi service in Phoenix in 2020 but only in a geofenced area under specific weather conditions — SAE Level 4, not Level 5. Cruise, Argo AI (shut down 2022), and other major programs failed to reach Level 5. The long-tail problem — handling rare edge cases safely — remained unsolved. NHTSA reported continued AV crashes and interventions.
ground_truth_notes: Hostile survival was critical here — adversarial testing in rain, snow, construction zones, and unusual pedestrian behavior routinely caused system failures. The geofenced test conditions did not generalize to unrestricted urban environments. The claim's generalization requirement (any condition, any urban environment) was testably false at the time of the evidence available.
sources: RAND Corporation 2016, 'Autonomous Vehicle Technology: A Guide for Policymakers' (Kalra & Paddock)
California DMV Autonomous Vehicle Disengagement Reports 2016-2021
SAE International J3016 Level of Driving Automation Standard (2016 revision)
Waymo Safety Report 2020 — Waymo One Phoenix launch documentation
NHTSA Standing General Order AV Crash Reporting 2021-2024
Musk Tesla Autonomy Investor Day prediction (October 2016, cited widely)

hypothesis: Blockchain-based supply chain tracking will achieve mainstream enterprise adoption (>30% of Fortune 500 companies using it operationally) by 2023.
evidence_available: IBM Food Trust (Walmart, Carrefour collaboration) launched 2018 tracking food provenance on Hyperledger Fabric. Maersk-IBM TradeLens shipping blockchain announced 2018. De Beers Tracr diamond tracking operational 2018. Gartner Hype Cycle 2018 placed blockchain near peak of inflated expectations. Deloitte 2018 survey: 74% of tech executives saw blockchain as a compelling use case. However: most pilots were small-scale. Interoperability between competing blockchain platforms was unsolved. ROI evidence was anecdotal. Legacy ERP integration costs were high. No independent peer-reviewed study had validated large-scale operational benefits over traditional databases.
known_outcome: Blockchain supply chain adoption fell well short of 30% Fortune 500 operational use by 2023. Maersk-IBM TradeLens was shut down in December 2022, citing inability to achieve commercial viability. IBM Food Trust scaled back significantly. Gartner moved blockchain to 'trough of disillusionment' by 2020. A 2023 Gartner survey found fewer than 10% of enterprises had blockchain in production for supply chain. Most pilots were abandoned or replaced by traditional database solutions with similar audit trail functionality.
ground_truth_notes: Verdict is UNRESOLVED because the evidence at decision time was genuinely ambiguous — there were real operational deployments and credible institutional backers, but insufficient replication and power to confirm the adoption trajectory claim. No KILL gates fired because measured values stayed above floors, but GO criteria were not met due to insufficient replication, power, and lack of CI excluding null.
sources: IBM Food Trust launch announcement and Walmart collaboration 2018 (IBM Press Release)
Maersk-IBM TradeLens launch 2018 and shutdown announcement December 2022
Gartner Hype Cycle for Blockchain Business 2018 and 2020
Deloitte Global Blockchain Survey 2018
Gartner Enterprise Blockchain Survey 2023
Wust & Gervais 2018, '2nd Crypto Valley Conference on Blockchain Technology' (when blockchain?)

hypothesis: Cloud gaming services (game streaming) will largely replace dedicated gaming consoles, capturing majority market share of gaming hardware spend by 2023.
evidence_available: Google Stadia announced March 2019, launching November 2019. Microsoft xCloud (Game Pass Ultimate) and NVIDIA GeForce NOW in beta. Netflix model applied to gaming was compelling in analyst reports. Newzoo projected cloud gaming market reaching $4.8B by 2023. Sony PlayStation Now had ~700K subscribers in 2019. Latency had dropped to 30-50ms on fiber broadband — approaching playability threshold. However: console generations remained dominant (PS5/Xbox Series X planned for 2020). Physical media still significant. Broadband penetration in gaming demographics was high but not universal. No platform had demonstrated mass consumer migration from console to cloud.
known_outcome: Cloud gaming did not replace consoles or capture majority hardware spend by 2023. Google Stadia was shut down in January 2023 after failing to attract sufficient subscribers. PS5 and Xbox Series X sold over 40M and 20M+ units respectively. Xbox Game Pass cloud gaming (xCloud) grew to ~30M subscribers by 2023 but was additive to, not replacing, console ownership. Physical console revenue still exceeded cloud gaming revenue. Cloud gaming remained a niche supplement rather than a replacement.
ground_truth_notes: UNRESOLVED at decision time because evidence genuinely did not point clearly either way — there were real technological and market signals in both directions. The claim did not reach KILL because hostile survival and generalization stayed above floors. GO was not reached due to insufficient replication, power, and lack of CI excluding null. The actual outcome was a KILL of the hypothesis, but the evidence available in June 2019 was too sparse to confidently kill it.
sources: Newzoo Cloud Gaming Revenue Forecast 2019
Google Stadia announcement GDC March 2019 and shutdown January 2023 (Google Blog)
Microsoft xCloud beta launch 2019
Sony PlayStation Now subscriber count Q4 2019 (Sony Investor Relations)
Xbox Game Pass subscriber data 2023 (Microsoft)
IDC Worldwide Quarterly Gaming Tracker 2019-2023

hypothesis: Solid-state lithium batteries will achieve commercial-scale production for electric vehicles by 2023, delivering energy density >400 Wh/kg and enabling 500+ mile EV range at mainstream price points.
evidence_available: Toyota announced solid-state battery EV target for 2020-2025 (later revised). QuantumScape (backed by Volkswagen) went public in 2020, claiming breakthrough in lithium-metal solid-state cells. Samsung SDI and CATL announced solid-state development programs. Lab demonstrations showed energy densities of 400-500 Wh/kg at coin-cell scale. However: scaling from lab to manufacturing was unproven — no company had produced solid-state cells in pouch/prismatic format at volume. Interface resistance at room temperature remained a significant challenge. Independent replication of manufacturer claims was limited to internal lab data. Manufacturing cost projections were highly speculative.
known_outcome: No solid-state battery achieved commercial-scale EV production by 2023. QuantumScape reported continued technical setbacks with cycle life and manufacturing scale-up through 2022-2023. Toyota revised its solid-state EV timeline to 2027-2028. The best-performing lithium-ion batteries in production EVs (e.g., Tesla 4680 cells) reached approximately 300 Wh/kg. Solid-state batteries remained a laboratory and limited prototype technology as of 2023, with commercial viability pushed to 2027-2030 by most analysts.
ground_truth_notes: UNRESOLVED because the evidence available in mid-2020 was genuinely mixed — laboratory results were real and promising, but manufacturing scale-up had not been attempted at relevant volumes. No KILL gates fired: effect was measured and above floor, hostile survival above 0.34, confound control above 0.20, and generalization was not required. GO was not met because replication was limited to internal lab data (0.40, just short of 0.5 threshold), power was insufficient, and CI did not exclude null for commercial-scale performance.
sources: QuantumScape S-1 filing and 2020 technical results presentation (SEC EDGAR 2020)
Toyota Motor Corporation Press Release on solid-state battery roadmap 2020
Janek & Zeier 2016, Nature Energy (solid-state battery challenges review)
CATL and Samsung SDI solid-state battery announcements 2020 (Reuters/Nikkei)
BloombergNEF Battery Price Survey 2020 (lithium-ion cost trajectory)
Solid Power and BMW partnership announcement 2020

hypothesis: Fiber-to-the-premises (FTTP) broadband delivers substantially superior reliability, speed, and latency compared to coaxial cable (HFC) networks for residential internet, making it the preferred infrastructure technology.
evidence_available: Google Fiber deployments (2012-2015) provided large-scale real-world comparison data. FCC Measuring Broadband America reports 2013-2014 showed fiber ISPs (FIOS, Google Fiber) consistently delivering 100%+ of advertised speeds vs. 80-90% for cable. Latency measurements: fiber 5-12ms vs. cable 20-30ms in independent tests. Ofcom UK 2014 report showed FTTP outperforming VDSL/cable on all measured dimensions. Academic studies (Stocker et al.) confirmed physical layer advantages. Independent replications by multiple national regulators. No confounds identified — physics of light transmission vs. electrical signal in coaxial cable was well understood.
known_outcome: Fiber-to-the-premises has been consistently demonstrated to outperform coaxial cable on all measured performance dimensions across multiple countries, regulatory bodies, and independent studies through 2015 and beyond. The UK, EU, South Korea, Japan, and US regulatory agencies all confirmed FTTP superiority in national broadband measurement programs. FTTP became the internationally recognized gold standard for fixed broadband infrastructure, with EU Digital Agenda and US BEAD program both prioritizing fiber deployment.
ground_truth_notes: Strong GO verdict. The hypothesis about physical infrastructure superiority was testable and had been tested rigorously. Multiple independent regulatory bodies (FCC, Ofcom, ARCEP France, TKK Germany) ran standardized measurements confirming the claim. Large sample sizes, consistent effects across geographies, and no plausible confounds. The physics underlying the claim (photons vs. electrons in bounded media) provided strong mechanistic support.
sources: FCC Measuring Broadband America Fixed Broadband Report 2014
Ofcom Connected Nations Report 2014 (UK broadband performance)
Google Fiber technical performance data 2013-2015 (FCC filing)
Stocker et al. 2017, Telecommunications Policy (FTTP vs HFC comparative analysis)
ARCEP 2014 Annual Broadband Barometer (France fiber vs. cable measurements)
Akamai State of the Internet Report Q4 2014 (fiber ISP performance rankings)

hypothesis: Juicero's proprietary $699 connected juice-pressing hardware provides sufficient value over hand-squeezing its own pre-packaged produce packs to command premium consumer adoption at scale.
evidence_available: At decision time: Juicero had raised $120M from top-tier VCs (Google Ventures, Campbell Soup). Device launched at $699 (later $399). Produce packs required a subscription. No independent consumer demand studies published. Internal metrics (DAU, retention) not disclosed. Early adopters were health-enthusiasts in major metros. No competitive benchmarking vs hand-squeezing published. Unit economics and CAC not disclosed. Consumer electronics hardware market penetration for premium appliances had historically been very thin (Juicero analogous to Keurig but higher price point with no lock-in advantage).
known_outcome: Bloomberg reported in April 2017 that Juicero packs could be hand-squeezed as effectively as the machine. Adoption collapsed. Juicero shut down in September 2017, less than two years after launch, returning remaining inventory money to subscribers. The hardware added no functional value beyond the packs themselves.
ground_truth_notes: The core value proposition (hardware necessity) was disproved by a simple adversarial test — hand-squeezing the packs. Effect below floor because hardware provided no measurable incremental output. HostileSurvival below floor because any independent test of the device's necessity falsified the claim. Classic case of VC-funded solution in search of a problem.
sources: Evans & Ramsey 2017, Bloomberg ('Juicero's Press Doesn't Actually Squeeze the Juice')
Primack 2017, Fortune ('Juicero Is Shutting Down')
Griffith 2017, New York Times (Juicero shutdown coverage)

hypothesis: The Segway personal transporter will transform urban transportation by replacing walking and short-distance driving, achieving mass adoption as a primary first/last-mile vehicle.
evidence_available: At decision time: Steve Jobs reportedly said it would be bigger than the PC. Jeff Bezos and John Doerr invested. Early prototypes demonstrated by Dean Kamen showed the self-balancing technology worked. No demand studies in real urban contexts. Regulatory status unclear — sidewalk vs road ambiguity in most jurisdictions. Price point ~$5,000 was not publicly confirmed but rumored high. No independent market sizing study published. Competitor analysis (bicycles, scooters) not conducted publicly. City infrastructure requirements (charging, parking) unaddressed.
known_outcome: Segway sold approximately 30,000 units in its first 5 years vs. a projected 10,000/week. Never achieved mass adoption. Total sales over 21 years were under 140,000 units. Consumer division sold to Ninebot in 2015; Segway Inc. shut down consumer production in 2020. Urban transportation was not transformed. The $5,000 price, regulatory barriers, and sidewalk bans eliminated mass adoption potential.
ground_truth_notes: The generalization claim (transform urban transport broadly across cities and demographics) required evidence across regulatory environments, price sensitivities, and infrastructure contexts — none of which had been measured at decision time. HostileSurvival below floor because the device failed in nearly every hostile real-world context (regulation, price resistance, range, weather).
sources: Kemper 2003, 'Code Name Ginger' (Harvard Business School Press)
Naughton 2020, Newsweek ('Why the Segway Failed')
Stewart 2015, The New Yorker ('Reinventing the Wheel')

hypothesis: Clubhouse's live audio drop-in social platform represents durable product-market fit and will sustain user engagement and growth beyond pandemic-era conditions.
evidence_available: At decision time (Jan 2021): Clubhouse had 2M weekly active users (up from 600K in Dec 2020), raised at $1B valuation. Celebrity appearances (Elon Musk) drove spikes. iOS-only, invite-only created artificial scarcity and buzz. No published retention cohort data. No revenue model. Competitors (Twitter Spaces, Facebook Live Audio) announced. Prior social audio (Anchor, Stereo) had failed to scale. No evidence of engagement outside pandemic context. No published churn metrics.
known_outcome: Downloads collapsed from 9.6M in February 2021 to under 1M/month by May 2021 as lockdowns eased and competitors launched. Twitter Spaces and Spotify Greenroom captured marginal users. Clubhouse laid off most staff by 2023 and pivoted to small-group audio. Never established durable DAU or monetization. Raised at $4B in April 2021 but growth never recovered.
ground_truth_notes: HostileSurvival below floor: when pandemic conditions were relaxed (the adversarial test), engagement collapsed immediately. Generalization below floor: the hypothesis required the platform to work broadly across non-pandemic, non-invite, non-iOS contexts — all of which it failed. A classic confound (lockdowns driving captive audience) was not controlled.
sources: Newton 2021, The Verge ('What happened to Clubhouse?')
Frier 2021, Bloomberg (Clubhouse growth reversal coverage)
Perez 2021, TechCrunch ('Clubhouse downloads have fallen 72% since February peak')

hypothesis: Pandemic-era demand for Peloton connected fitness hardware represents a durable structural shift in consumer exercise behavior that will sustain elevated revenue post-pandemic.
evidence_available: At decision time: Peloton had grown revenue 172% YoY in fiscal Q1 2021. Stock had risen 440% in 2020. Demand exceeded supply — waitlists of 8-12 weeks. Management announced $400M factory investment and projected sustained demand. No independent demand elasticity study. No evidence distinguishing COVID-driven captive demand from structural preference shift. Gym closure was confound for Peloton usage. Prior home exercise equipment market had boom-bust pattern (NordicTrack 1990s). No churn cohort data published.
known_outcome: After vaccine rollout and gym reopenings in mid-2021, demand collapsed. Peloton reported a $376M loss in Q2 fiscal 2022. CEO John Foley resigned February 2022. Manufacturing investment was written down; the company paused factory construction. Stock fell ~90% from its peak. The 'structural shift' thesis was rejected; demand was largely pandemic-driven substitution.
ground_truth_notes: The hypothesis required generalization across post-pandemic consumer behavior — which was the exact condition that disproved it. Effect below floor because the net durable demand signal was essentially zero after gym reopening. The confound (forced gym closures) was the primary driver, not genuine preference change. HostileSurvival below floor because gym reopening (the natural adversarial test) immediately destroyed the demand signal.
sources: Thomas & Cao 2022, Wall Street Journal ('Peloton CEO Steps Down')
Fitzgerald 2022, CNBC (Peloton demand collapse reporting)
Del Rey 2021, Vox/Recode ('The Peloton bubble')

hypothesis: Snapchat's Stories feature (chronological 24-hour photo/video sequences visible to all followers) will drive a meaningful increase in daily active engagement and content creation volume on the platform.
evidence_available: At decision time: Stories launched October 2013. Snapchat had ~26M daily active users pre-launch. Internal engagement metrics showed 30-50M Snaps per day. The Stories format addressed a clear user need: sharing moments without the permanence pressure of Facebook. Prior evidence that ephemeral content drove high engagement (main Snapchat product had proven this). No published external study, but platform engagement data was internally strong. Adjacent formats (Instagram video, Vine) showed demand for short-form visual content creation.
known_outcome: Stories became the dominant content format on Snapchat, growing to 100M daily viewers by 2015. The format was so successful that Instagram copied it exactly in 2016 (Instagram Stories), followed by WhatsApp, Facebook, and YouTube. Stories drove Snapchat's growth to 100M+ DAU by 2016. The format became the dominant social media content paradigm of the late 2010s.
ground_truth_notes: Snapchat Stories is one of the most validated product hypotheses in social media history — validated not only by Snapchat's own engagement metrics but by the universal copying by every major competitor. The format generalized across platforms, demographics, and geographies. Effect was large, replication was strong (every major platform independently validated demand), and confound control was reasonable (user growth tracked independently of other product changes).
sources: Gallagher 2018, 'How to Turn Down a Billion Dollars: The Snapchat Story' (St. Martin's Press)
Systrom 2016, Instagram blog post ('Instagram Stories')
Kokalitcheva 2016, Fortune ('Snapchat is about to reveal some important data about its users')

hypothesis: DoorDash's marketplace model can achieve sustainable unit economics at scale, with contribution margin turning positive as order volume grows and operational density increases in key markets.
evidence_available: At decision time: DoorDash had ~35% US food delivery market share (up from 18% in 2018), raising $600M at $7.1B valuation. Internal claims of contribution margin improvement in dense markets (Chicago, San Francisco). Competitor Grubhub had demonstrated profitable unit economics in mature markets. No published independent audited unit economics. Gig worker cost structure debated — AB5 regulatory risk in California unresolved. DoorDash burning >$400M/year. No SEC disclosures (pre-IPO). Restaurant commission structures (15-30%) under political scrutiny.
known_outcome: DoorDash IPO'd December 2020 and disclosed S-1 financials showing improving but still negative contribution margins. By 2022-2023, DoorDash reached Marketplace GOV of $53B and reported its first full-year GAAP profitability approaching in 2023 (Adjusted EBITDA positive). The density hypothesis proved partially correct in mature markets but required far longer timelines and more capital than projected in 2019.
ground_truth_notes: At decision time (2019), the unit economics hypothesis was genuinely UNRESOLVED — not enough audited data, replication across markets, or time series evidence to confirm or deny. Support score reflects early positive signals but insufficient evidence for GO. The outcome was ultimately directionally correct but required many more years to validate.
sources: DoorDash S-1 Filing 2020, SEC EDGAR
Dignan 2019, TechCrunch ('DoorDash raises $600 million at $12.6 billion valuation')
Creswell 2020, New York Times ('How DoorDash's road to an IPO is paved with restaurant fees')

hypothesis: Zoom's cloud-native video conferencing architecture can reliably serve enterprise-scale deployments with superior reliability, ease of use, and cross-platform performance compared to incumbent solutions (Cisco WebEx, Microsoft Skype).
evidence_available: At decision time (April 2019, IPO): Zoom had 344 customers contributing >$100K ARR. NPS consistently above 60 (industry-leading). Revenue grew 118% YoY to $331M. Customer retention rate >139% net dollar retention. Independent analyst benchmarks (Gartner, Forrester) ranked Zoom #1 in ease of use. Enterprise wins at Uber, Stanford, Slack itself. G2 Crowd reviews showed superior reliability scores vs WebEx and Skype for Business. Architecture advantages (global co-location of data centers, client-server split) documented in technical press.
known_outcome: Zoom became the defining enterprise communications platform of the 2020s. Revenue grew from $622M (FY2020) to $4.1B (FY2022). During COVID-19, Zoom scaled from 10M to 300M daily meeting participants in 3 months — the ultimate stress test of enterprise-scale reliability. Maintained >130% net dollar retention through 2022. The architecture hypothesis was validated at unprecedented scale.
ground_truth_notes: At IPO (April 2019), Zoom had unusually strong pre-GO evidence: multiple independent analyst rankings, high NPS, documented net dollar retention, and wins in enterprise accounts that had tried competitors. The COVID stress test (300M concurrent users) provided the largest-ever real-world validation of a video conferencing architecture claim.
sources: Zoom Video Communications S-1 IPO Filing 2019, SEC EDGAR
Gartner Magic Quadrant for Meeting Solutions 2018 (Zoom leader ranking)
Yuan 2020, Zoom Blog ('A Message to Our Users') — architecture scaling documentation

hypothesis: Slack's channel-based team messaging platform will displace email as the primary internal communication tool in software-driven enterprises, achieving durable enterprise stickiness and expanding net dollar retention.
evidence_available: At decision time: Slack had 2.3M daily active users (up from 500K at launch in Aug 2013). $200M ARR run rate growing 3.5x YoY. 370,000 paying users. NPS >50. Retention and expansion metrics showed >110% net dollar retention. Won accounts at Airbnb, NASA, BuzzFeed, IBM pilot programs. Independent user surveys showed high satisfaction and reduced email volume in adopting teams. No published controlled study on enterprise-wide email reduction. Channel-based model was differentiated from HipChat (Atlassian) and Microsoft Lync.
known_outcome: Slack grew to $902M ARR by FY2021 and was acquired by Salesforce for $27.7B in July 2021. Became the dominant internal communication tool for software companies. Microsoft responded with Teams (2017), validating the market. By 2022, Slack had 18M daily active users. The enterprise stickiness hypothesis was validated — net dollar retention remained above 125% through 2020.
ground_truth_notes: By early 2016, Slack had multiple independent signals: high NPS, strong retention, rapid viral enterprise growth, and wins across diverse verticals. The generalization to enterprise broadly (not just startups) was validated by IBM, NASA, and large corporate pilots. Microsoft's competitive response (Teams) is the strongest possible industry validation of market hypothesis.
sources: Griffith 2015, Wired ('The Most Fascinating Profile You'll Ever Read About a Guy and His Boring Startup')
Slack S-1 Filing 2019, SEC EDGAR (historical ARR and retention data)
Salesforce press release July 2021 (Slack acquisition close)

hypothesis: Airbnb's peer-to-peer home-sharing marketplace can achieve sustainable demand at scale by providing travelers with authentic, affordable accommodation alternatives while giving hosts meaningful supplemental income — overcoming the trust barrier to staying in strangers' homes.
evidence_available: At decision time: Airbnb had 120,000 listings and 4M room-nights booked in 2011 (10x from 2010). Operating in 89 countries. $119M Series B raised at $1.3B valuation (Andreessen Horowitz, Sequoia). NPS data internally positive. Host repeat rates high. A/B testing showed profile photos and review systems were critical trust mechanisms. Guest satisfaction surveys showed high satisfaction rates. Incident rates low (though 'Ransackgate' in summer 2011 had been addressed with Host Guarantee program). Regulatory landscape unclear but not yet hostile.
known_outcome: Airbnb grew to 6M+ active listings in 100,000+ cities by 2020. Revenue reached $3.4B in 2019 (pre-COVID). IPO in December 2020 at $47B valuation, one of the largest hospitality sector valuations ever. Trust mechanisms (reviews, ID verification, Host Guarantee) proved sufficient for mass adoption. The shared economy hypothesis was validated at scale — overcoming the trust barrier was achievable through platform design.
ground_truth_notes: By early 2012, Airbnb had enough multi-market replication (89 countries), growth trajectory, and trust-mechanism validation to support a GO. The 10x YoY growth, high NPS, and successful trust-barrier solutions (review system, Host Guarantee) provided multi-faceted confirmation. Andreessen Horowitz's thesis (published in blog posts) articulated the evidence rationale clearly.
sources: Andreessen 2011, Andreessen Horowitz blog (Airbnb investment thesis)
Gallagher 2017, 'The Airbnb Story' (Houghton Mifflin Harcourt)
Airbnb IPO S-1 Filing 2020, SEC EDGAR

hypothesis: Dropbox's freemium cloud file-sync product can convert a large fraction of free users to paid plans in both consumer and enterprise segments, creating durable ARR growth through viral bottom-up adoption.
evidence_available: At decision time: Dropbox had 50M registered users (up from 4M in 2010), with referral program driving 60% of all signups. Freemium conversion rate ~4% (above SaaS benchmarks). $250M Series B at $4B valuation (2011). Consumer NPS extremely high. Early enterprise traction (businesses using personal accounts had emerged organically). File sync technology worked across Mac, Windows, iOS, Android. No published independent churn analysis. Competitors (Google Drive, Microsoft SkyDrive) not yet launched at scale. Box targeting pure enterprise segment.
known_outcome: Dropbox reached 500M registered users and $1.1B revenue by IPO in March 2018. Freemium-to-paid conversion and business plan (Dropbox Business) drove ARR. IPO at $9.2B valuation. The viral referral model and freemium conversion hypothesis were validated. However, sustained growth post-IPO was challenged by commodity cloud storage competition from Google and Microsoft.
ground_truth_notes: By 2012, Dropbox had unusually strong evidence for a GO: viral referral growth (60% of signups from referral), above-benchmark freemium conversion, 10x user growth in 2 years, and cross-platform adoption confirming the generalization claim. The referral program A/B tests (documented by Houston) provided replication of the viral growth mechanism.
sources: Houston 2010, Dropbox blog ('How we grew Dropbox to 4 million users in 15 months')
Dropbox S-1 Filing 2018, SEC EDGAR
Olsen 2015, 'The Lean Product Playbook' (Wiley) — Dropbox case study

hypothesis: Robinhood's commission-free mobile-first brokerage will successfully democratize stock market investing by drawing in previously excluded young and lower-income retail investors who were deterred by trading fees.
evidence_available: At decision time: Robinhood had 1M users on waitlist pre-launch and 1M+ accounts by end of 2015. Average account holder age ~26 (vs ~50 for traditional brokers). No published independent study of whether new users were truly new-to-investing vs. fee-sensitive switchers. Revenue model (payment for order flow, interest on cash) not fully disclosed or scrutinized. Gamification features (confetti, streaks) not yet identified as potentially harmful. Regulatory environment for PFOF not yet challenged. No published data on user investment outcomes or trading behavior relative to benchmarks.
known_outcome: Robinhood grew to 22M funded accounts by 2021 IPO. Undeniably broadened retail participation. However, the 'democratization' hypothesis faced hostile scrutiny: PFOF means Robinhood users received worse execution prices than discount brokers; gamification drove excessive trading (Welch 2022 shows Robinhood users traded 40x more than Schwab users); the 2021 GameStop episode revealed platform risk through trading halts. The hypothesis was partially confirmed (more users) but the quality/benefit dimension remains contested.
ground_truth_notes: UNRESOLVED at 2016 decision date: user growth signal was strong (effect measurable, ciExcludesNull=true) but the core democratization question — are these users better off, and are they truly new to investing? — lacked independent replication and hostile test survival. The hypothesis required generalization across diverse user outcomes, which was unmeasured. Actual outcome confirmed partial hypothesis (access) while partially refuting it (outcomes).
sources: Wurgler & Welch 2022, NBER Working Paper ('The Reddit Rebellion')
Robinhood S-1 Filing 2021, SEC EDGAR
Bhattacharya et al. 2021, Journal of Finance (retail investor trading behavior study)

hypothesis: Twitter under Elon Musk's ownership can retain major brand advertiser revenue while significantly reducing content moderation, on the theory that advertisers prioritize reach and engagement over brand safety concerns.
evidence_available: At decision time (Nov 2022, one week post-acquisition): Musk had reinstated previously banned accounts. Content moderation team reduced by ~50% in first week. Musk claimed advertisers would stay due to platform's unique reach (~250M monetizable DAU). Internal Twitter projections reportedly showed advertiser confidence declining. IPG (large ad holding company) recommended clients pause Twitter spend. Nonprofit Sleeping Giants had been successfully pressuring advertisers on moderation issues since 2016 — documented historical precedent. No published evidence that advertisers had tolerated significant moderation rollbacks on other platforms.
known_outcome: Advertising revenue fell ~50% YoY in Q4 2022. Major brands (Apple, General Mills, Audi, GM, United Airlines) paused spending immediately post-acquisition. By 2023, Twitter (rebranded X) had lost >50% of its top 1000 advertisers. Musk acknowledged a $3B annual revenue shortfall attributable primarily to advertiser flight. The hypothesis was decisively falsified within 90 days.
ground_truth_notes: Effect below floor: the measurable effect on advertiser retention was strongly negative, not positive. HostileSurvival below floor: the first major hostile test (IPG recommendation to pause) immediately triggered a cascade of departures — the hypothesis failed every real-world test within 60 days. Historical precedent (Sleeping Giants campaigns 2016-2020) provided strong prior evidence that brand safety was non-negotiable for major advertisers.
sources: Vranica & Tilley 2022, Wall Street Journal ('Twitter Advertisers Flee After Musk Takeover')
Musk 2022, Twitter post (advertiser retention claims, Nov 2022)
Frenkel & Conger 2022, New York Times (Twitter advertiser exodus reporting)

hypothesis: Pinterest can successfully convert its high purchase-intent visual discovery user base into a meaningful e-commerce platform generating substantial transaction or advertising revenue from product-linked content.
evidence_available: At decision time: Pinterest had 416M MAU (growing rapidly during COVID lockdowns). Internal data showed 89% of users use Pinterest for purchase inspiration. Launched Pinterest Shopping (shoppable pins, product catalogs) in 2019. Average order value from Pinterest referrals was $50+ (above other social platforms). Q1 2020 revenue grew 35% YoY to $272M but primarily advertising. No published conversion rate data for shoppable pins. Competitor Instagram had launched Shopping in 2018 with mixed results. User base skewed female (70%), high household income — attractive to advertisers. No independent transaction volume data.
known_outcome: Pinterest grew revenue from $1.1B (2020) to $2.8B (2022) primarily through advertising, not direct commerce transactions. Shoppable pins drove meaningful traffic but Pinterest did not become a primary transaction platform — it remained primarily a discovery/advertising platform. The 'meaningful e-commerce platform' hypothesis was partially validated for advertising but not for transaction-based commerce. 2022-2024 saw renewed e-commerce push under new CEO Bill Ready (ex-Google Commerce).
ground_truth_notes: UNRESOLVED at mid-2020: strong purchase intent signal (effect measurable) but no replication of the conversion-to-transaction hypothesis across categories, no CI exclusion of null on transaction conversion rates, and power below GO threshold. The claim required generalization (that visual discovery intent would translate to actual purchases at scale) which was not yet established. The outcome remained genuinely mixed.
sources: Pinterest S-1 Filing 2019, SEC EDGAR
Frier 2020, Bloomberg ('Pinterest Wants to Be the Next Big Commerce Platform')
Pinterest Q1 2020 Earnings Release (revenue and MAU data)

hypothesis: Snap's Spectacles camera-equipped sunglasses will achieve meaningful consumer adoption as a wearable social content creation device, establishing Snap as a hardware company alongside its software platform.
evidence_available: At decision time: Spectacles launched November 2016 via limited vending machines (SnapBots). Hype driven by scarcity marketing — lines outside machines.
