Media coverage extremely positive. $129.99 price point considered accessible. Snap internally projected significant hardware revenue for 2017. No published pre-order conversion data. Historical context: Google Glass had failed in 2014 (regulatory, social, price issues). No independent user research on whether video recorded from Spectacles would be shared more than phone video. Limited distribution (vending machines only) prevented mass testing.
known_outcome: Snap produced ~220,000 pairs in 2016-2017 and could not sell them — resulting in $40M inventory write-down reported in Q3 2017. Daily use among purchasers was extremely low (most users used them once or twice). Spectacles 2 (2018) and Spectacles 3 (2019) also failed commercially. Spectacles 4 AR (2021) pivoted to developer/creator tool. Consumer adoption hypothesis was definitively falsified.
ground_truth_notes: Effect below floor: actual adoption and repeat usage rate was effectively zero outside initial novelty. HostileSurvival below floor: the first real-world commercial test (broad availability) revealed no demand beyond hype purchases. Generalization below floor: the device did not generalize beyond novelty use in any demographic segment. The Google Glass failure (2014) provided directly relevant prior evidence that was ignored.
sources: Snap Inc. Q3 2017 Earnings Call (inventory write-down disclosure)
Kastrenakes 2017, The Verge ('Snap is stuck with $40 million worth of unsold Spectacles')
Kuchler 2017, Financial Times (Spectacles commercial failure reporting)

hypothesis: Getaround's peer-to-peer car sharing marketplace (renting personal vehicles by the hour) can achieve sufficient liquidity and utilization in urban markets to create a viable alternative to traditional car rental and car ownership for urban residents.
evidence_available: At decision time: Getaround raised $45M Series C (2016) and $300M round (2019 announced). Operating in ~300 US cities. Toyota invested $10M in 2016. Average vehicle earning ~$500/month for hosts. Platform had 4M users. Turo (competitor) showing similar growth trajectory. No published independent liquidity study by market. Regulatory status in some cities unclear. Insurance product (developed with Markel) was key enabling innovation. No published contribution margin data. Market context: Zipcar had validated hourly car sharing demand but with fleet model.
known_outcome: Getaround merged with Drivy (European car sharing, 2019) to expand internationally. However, growth remained limited — the company struggled with liquidity in smaller markets and vehicle damage/dispute issues that degraded host retention. Filed for bankruptcy protection in 2023 after failing to raise additional capital. The marketplace viability hypothesis proved UNRESOLVED: works in dense urban markets but failed to achieve sustainable economics at scale.
ground_truth_notes: UNRESOLVED at 2018 decision date: there was a genuine positive demand signal (effect measurable at 0.48) and Zipcar had validated the hourly car rental model (providing some replication of demand), but the P2P marketplace liquidity hypothesis (different from fleet ownership) lacked independent replication, CI exclusion, and sufficient power at decision time. The hypothesis required generalization to diverse city densities — which eventually failed in thinner markets.
sources: Kokalitcheva 2016, Fortune ('Toyota invests in car-sharing startup Getaround')
Lomas 2019, TechCrunch ('Getaround acquires European rival Drivy')
Slotnick 2023, Business Insider (Getaround bankruptcy filing coverage)

hypothesis: SPACs (Special Purpose Acquisition Companies) outperform traditional IPOs for retail investors on a 1-year post-merger return basis.
evidence_available: SPACs raised a record $13.6B in 2019 and were gaining mainstream attention. Proponents cited faster time-to-market, price certainty via PIPE investors, and retail investor access. Early 2019-2020 data showed a handful of SPAC mergers posting large short-term gains (DraftKings, Nikola). Academic research on SPAC performance pre-2020 was limited to Klausner & Ohlrogge 2020 (NBER), which found negative post-merger drifts but was contested. Sponsor economics (founder shares, warrants) were understood to be dilutive but the magnitude was debated. Lock-up expirations for PIPE investors created known selling pressure. No large pre-registered return study existed; most evidence was anecdotal or from small samples of a favorable period.
known_outcome: SPACs dramatically underperformed traditional IPOs for retail investors. Klausner, Ohlrogge & Ruan (2022, Journal of Finance) found median 1-year post-merger returns of -65% for SPAC investors who held through completion. Over 90% of 2020-2021 vintage SPACs traded below their $10 NAV by 2022. The SEC tightened SPAC disclosure rules in 2022-2023. The SPAC market collapsed: only 13 SPAC IPOs in Q4 2022 vs. 298 in Q4 2020.
ground_truth_notes: The large measured effect for early high-profile SPACs was driven by selection bias (cherry-picking winners) and a bull market tailwind. Once controlling for sponsor dilution and lock-up selling, the measured net effect on retail investors was negative. The hostile-test survival was low because the dilution math was straightforward once modeled — critics identified the structural flaw at launch.
sources: Klausner, Ohlrogge & Ruan 2022, Journal of Finance — A Sober Look at SPACs
Gahng, Ritter & Zhang 2021, SSRN — SPACs
SEC Staff Bulletin 2022, Special Purpose Acquisition Companies

hypothesis: Passive index funds outperform actively managed funds on net risk-adjusted returns for retail investors over 10+ year periods.
evidence_available: By 2000, Sharpe's 1991 arithmetic of active management (zero-sum before costs) was well-established. Jensen (1968) showed mutual funds underperform their benchmarks on average net of fees. Malkiel (1973, A Random Walk Down Wall Street) had argued for indexing for decades. The S&P SPIVA scorecards (launched 2002) were in development; precursor data from Gruber (1996) showed 80% of active funds underperform. Vanguard's index funds had 25 years of data. Fama & French (1992, 1993) provided a risk-factor framework that explained most active manager returns. Survivorship bias in mutual fund databases was documented (Brown & Goetzmann 1995).
known_outcome: Confirmed decisively. S&P SPIVA 2022 report showed 90%+ of active large-cap U.S. funds underperformed the S&P 500 over 20 years. Morningstar's Active/Passive Barometer consistently documents the same pattern across asset classes and geographies. Warren Buffett won his famous 10-year $1M bet (2008-2017) that the S&P 500 index would outperform a hedge fund portfolio. Assets in passive vehicles exceeded active U.S. equity funds for the first time in 2019.
ground_truth_notes: This is one of the most robustly confirmed hypotheses in finance. The arithmetic argument (Sharpe 1991) is unfalsifiable in theory; the empirical record across decades, geographies, and asset classes is overwhelming. The effect is driven primarily by fee drag and trading costs, not manager skill per se.
sources: Sharpe 1991, Financial Analysts Journal — The Arithmetic of Active Management
Jensen 1968, Journal of Finance — The Performance of Mutual Funds
Gruber 1996, Journal of Finance — Another Puzzle: The Growth in Actively Managed Mutual Funds
S&P SPIVA U.S. Scorecard 2022
Morningstar 2023, US Active-Passive Barometer

hypothesis: ESG-screened equity portfolios deliver superior risk-adjusted returns (higher Sharpe ratio) compared to conventional portfolios over 5+ year periods.
evidence_available: By early 2018, ESG AUM had grown to ~$23 trillion globally (GSIA 2018). Multiple meta-analyses (Friede, Busch & Bassen 2015) claimed 90% of studies showed neutral or positive ESG-financial performance relationship, but these mixed ESG ratings with return data inconsistently. Khan, Serafeim & Yoon (2016, Accounting Review) showed materiality-weighted ESG scores predicted returns. However, ESG ratings across providers (MSCI, Sustainalytics, ISS) diverged by >50% (Berg, Koelbel & Rigobon 2019, forthcoming). Factor exposures (quality, low-vol) explained much of observed ESG alpha in Fama-French frameworks. No pre-registered large-sample study existed. The 2015-2017 outperformance of ESG indexes was largely driven by sector tilts (overweight tech, underweight energy).
known_outcome: Mixed and period-dependent. ESG funds broadly outperformed in 2017-2021 (tech-heavy portfolios) but significantly underperformed in 2022 when energy stocks surged. Pedersen, Fitzgibbons & Pomorski (2021, Journal of Financial Economics) showed ESG alpha largely explained by known risk factors. Berg et al. (2022, Review of Finance) found ESG ratings disagreement undermines portfolio construction. Morningstar 2023 data showed majority of ESG funds underperformed their conventional benchmarks over 5 years once sector tilts adjusted.
ground_truth_notes: The ESG outperformance hypothesis remains genuinely unresolved due to: (1) severe ESG ratings disagreement making hypothesis testing difficult, (2) evidence that most apparent alpha is factor-driven, (3) period sensitivity — the same funds that outperformed 2017-2021 underperformed 2022. CIs do not robustly exclude zero after factor adjustment.
sources: Friede, Busch & Bassen 2015, Journal of Sustainable Finance & Investment
Berg, Koelbel & Rigobon 2022, Review of Finance — Aggregate Confusion
Pedersen, Fitzgibbons & Pomorski 2021, Journal of Financial Economics
GSIA 2018 Global Sustainable Investment Review

hypothesis: Venture capital returns follow a power law: the top decile of investments returns more than the entire rest of a fund's portfolio combined.
evidence_available: By 2000, substantial LP data from Horsley Bridge (1985-2014 vintage data, covering $28B in investments) showed extreme return concentration. Sahlman (1990, Journal of Financial Economics) documented VC fund structure and return patterns. Gompers & Lerner (1997, 1999) analyzed 794 VC funds and showed return skewness. AngelList data (later) and Cambridge Associates returns showed consistent concentration. The Sand Hill Econometrics dataset tracked individual investment returns. Practitioner consensus (Sequoia, Benchmark, a16z) consistently described the power law as the organizing principle. The mathematical argument (one 100x return dominates ten 2x returns in a portfolio) was straightforward.
known_outcome: Confirmed. Andreessen Horowitz (2012) published analysis showing the top 6 companies in their portfolio (of ~40) returned more than all others combined. Cambridge Associates data through 2020 shows top decile VC funds return 5-10x while median funds return 1-1.5x. Correlation between a single investment's return and fund-level return is extremely high for top-returning companies. The power law structure has been replicated across geographies (Europe, Israel, Asia) and time periods.
ground_truth_notes: The power law in VC returns is one of the most robust empirical regularities in alternative finance. It holds across fund vintages, geographies, and fund sizes. The main debate is about degree of concentration, not existence of the phenomenon. Survivorship bias, while present, does not explain the magnitude of the top-1% outlier returns.
sources: Sahlman 1990, Journal of Financial Economics — The Structure and Governance of Venture Capital
Gompers & Lerner 1997, Journal of Finance — Risk and Reward in Private Equity Investments
Andreessen 2012, WSJ — In Defense of the Wounded Startup
Cambridge Associates 2021, US Venture Capital Index and Selected Benchmark Statistics
Horsley Bridge Partners LP data (cited in Thiel 2014, Zero to One)

hypothesis: NFTs (Non-Fungible Tokens) represent a durable store of value and a robust digital ownership mechanism for creators and collectors.
evidence_available: By mid-2021, NFT trading volume had surged to $2.5B in Q2 2021 (DappRadar). Beeple's 'Everydays' sold at Christie's for $69M in March 2021. Proponents cited: blockchain immutability, creator royalty enforcement, verifiable scarcity. Critics noted: (1) most NFT value was platform-dependent (OpenSea), (2) wash trading inflated volumes (Chainalysis estimated 15-20% of volume), (3) the underlying asset (JPEG) was copyable, (4) smart contract enforceability of royalties was untested, (5) no track record beyond 12-18 months of data. Academic literature was almost entirely absent. Market was dominated by speculative momentum narratives with virtually no DCF-based valuation framework.
known_outcome: The NFT market collapsed. Trading volume fell 97% from its 2021 peak by 2023 (DappRadar 2023). The vast majority of NFTs became illiquid or worthless: a dappGambl study (2023) found 95% of NFTs had zero floor price. Creator royalty enforcement collapsed as marketplaces (Blur) made royalties optional. Beeple's Everydays, had it been sold in 2023, would have fetched dramatically less. NFTs failed as a store of value by any conventional metric.
ground_truth_notes: Two KILL gates fire: (1) hostileSurvival < 0.34 — the claim did not survive adversarial analysis of wash trading, platform dependency, or royalty enforceability; (2) generalization < 0.34 — the claim asserted universal digital ownership but failed to generalize across platforms, time periods, or asset types. The short track record and absence of academic evidence made this hypothesis testable only in hindsight.
sources: DappRadar 2022, NFT Market Report
Chainalysis 2022, Crypto Crime Report — Wash Trading in NFTs
dappGambl 2023, Dead NFTs: The Evolving Landscape of the NFT Market
Nadini et al. 2021, Scientific Reports — Mapping the NFT Revolution

hypothesis: A systematic value investing strategy (buying stocks with low price-to-book ratios) consistently beats the market over rolling 10-year periods in the post-2010 environment.
evidence_available: Fama & French (1992, 1993) provided robust multi-decade evidence that HML (high-minus-low book-to-market) factor earned a positive premium. Lakonishok, Shleifer & Vishny (1994) confirmed value premium in contrarian strategies. By 2012, value had outperformed growth for decades in the academic record. However, behavioral and risk-based explanations for the value premium were contested. The Graham-Dodd tradition had strong practitioner support. Piotroski (2000) showed F-Score enhanced value strategies. Some evidence that the value premium had weakened post-publication (McLean & Pontiff 2016, forthcoming). Low interest rate environment in 2010s beginning to disadvantage value stocks by sector composition.
known_outcome: Value dramatically underperformed growth from 2010-2020 — the longest and deepest value drawdown in recorded history. The Russell 1000 Value index underperformed the Russell 1000 Growth index by over 200 percentage points over 2010-2020. AQR published extensively on the 'value crisis' (Asness 2020). Fama & French (2020, Critical Finance Review) acknowledged value premium had weakened. The COVID-19 crash briefly restored value outperformance in 2021-2022 (energy, financials) but the 10-year rolling premium remained near zero or negative.
ground_truth_notes: The 2012 decision date captures a genuine moment of uncertainty. The historical record supported value, but the forward-looking claim (consistent 10-year rolling outperformance) in the specific post-2010 period failed. However, this may be a regime-specific failure rather than a fundamental refutation — the underlying behavioral story remains intact. CI does not exclude zero for the post-2010 period specifically.
sources: Fama & French 1992, Journal of Finance — The Cross-Section of Expected Stock Returns
Lakonishok, Shleifer & Vishny 1994, Journal of Finance — Contrarian Investment, Extrapolation, and Risk
Asness et al. 2020, Journal of Portfolio Management — Is (Systematic) Value Investing Dead?
Fama & French 2020, Critical Finance Review — The Value Premium

hypothesis: High-frequency trading (HFT) generates net benefits for markets through improved liquidity provision, tighter bid-ask spreads, and faster price discovery.
evidence_available: By 2012, HFT accounted for ~50% of U.S. equity volume (Tabb Group). Hendershott, Jones & Menkveld (2011, Journal of Finance) showed NYSE algorithmic trading improved liquidity. Biais, Foucault & Moinas (2011) provided theoretical framework for HFT benefits. Spreads had fallen dramatically from $0.125 to $0.01 increments since decimalization. Critics: (1) Kirilenko et al. (2011) — HFT exacerbated the May 2010 Flash Crash; (2) HFT may withdraw liquidity in stressed markets (liquidity illusion); (3) speed advantage creates arms race with zero social value; (4) front-running institutional orders via latency arbitrage. CFTC/SEC had not completed investigations. Lewis's Flash Boys (2014) not yet published.
known_outcome: Genuinely mixed verdict in the academic literature. Jones (2013, Columbia Business School) concluded HFT generally improves markets; Menkveld & Yueshen (2019, Review of Financial Studies) found HFT liquidity evaporates in crises. The SEC's MIDAS data program (2012+) provided more granular evidence but produced mixed findings across market conditions. The consensus view is that HFT benefits (spreads, discovery speed) exist but are unevenly distributed and reverse in volatile periods, making the 'net beneficial' claim unresolved.
ground_truth_notes: UNRESOLVED is the correct characterization as of 2012 and arguably still today. The mixed evidence reflects genuine heterogeneity: HFT benefits are real but conditional on market conditions. Normal markets show spread compression; stress periods show liquidity withdrawal. No comprehensive welfare analysis has achieved academic consensus. CI does not robustly exclude zero for the composite net-benefit claim.
sources: Hendershott, Jones & Menkveld 2011, Journal of Finance — Does Algorithmic Trading Improve Liquidity?
Kirilenko et al. 2011, CFTC Working Paper — The Flash Crash
Jones 2013, Columbia Business School Working Paper — What Do We Know About HFT?
SEC 2014, Equity Market Structure Literature Review

hypothesis: Bitcoin functions as a reliable hedge against inflation, maintaining or increasing purchasing power when consumer price indices rise.
evidence_available: By January 2021, Bitcoin had surged from ~$7,000 to $30,000+ during a period of unprecedented Fed balance sheet expansion. Paul Tudor Jones (May 2020) and MicroStrategy (August 2020) publicly adopted Bitcoin as an inflation hedge, driving mainstream credibility. Pantera Capital and others cited Bitcoin's fixed 21M supply cap as the theoretical basis. Correlation with gold was cited as supporting evidence. However: (1) Bitcoin's short history (12 years) covered only one prior inflationary episode; (2) correlation with risk assets (Nasdaq) was high and rising; (3) the inflation-hedge narrative was primarily narrative-driven, not empirical; (4) Bitcoin's extreme volatility (90-day realized vol >80%) made it a poor practical hedge. Klein, Thu & Walther (2018) found Bitcoin not comparable to gold as a safe haven.
known_outcome: The claim failed. During the 2021-2022 inflation surge (CPI peaked at 9.1% in June 2022), Bitcoin fell from ~$69,000 (November 2021) to ~$16,000 (November 2022) — a 77% drawdown during the highest inflation in 40 years. Correlation with Nasdaq was near 0.7-0.9 during this period. Baur & Hoang (2021, Finance Research Letters) and subsequent empirical work found no statistically robust inflation-hedge property. Bitcoin behaved as a high-beta risk asset, not an inflation hedge.
ground_truth_notes: Two KILL gates fire: (1) hostileSurvival < 0.34 — the hypothesis failed its most basic adversarial test: Bitcoin dropped 77% during the highest inflation in 40 years; (2) generalization < 0.34 — the claim requires the hedge property to hold across inflationary regimes, which it demonstrably did not. The high correlation with risk assets was identifiable in the evidence structure as of January 2021.
sources: Klein, Thu & Walther 2018, Journal of International Financial Markets — Bitcoin is Not the New Gold
Baur & Hoang 2021, Finance Research Letters — Bitcoin and Gold in the Age of COVID-19
Tudor Jones 2020, BVI Market Outlook — The Great Monetary Inflation
Coinmetrics 2022, State of the Network — Bitcoin Correlation Study

hypothesis: Systematic quantitative/algorithmic trading strategies generate persistent, statistically significant alpha (excess returns above risk-adjusted benchmarks) over multi-year periods.
evidence_available: By 2010, Renaissance Technologies' Medallion Fund had reportedly returned ~66% annualized before fees from 1988-2018 (not yet fully documented). AQR, DE Shaw, and Two Sigma were reporting strong multi-year track records. Academic evidence: Jegadeesh & Titman (1993) momentum, Fama-French value, and Carhart (1997) showed exploitable factors. However: (1) most fund-level data was private; (2) survivorship bias in reported quant performance was severe; (3) factor crowding was emerging as a concern (Lo & MacKinlay 1990); (4) Harvey, Liu & Zhu (2016, forthcoming) would document 314 published factors with multiple-testing concerns; (5) many strategies showed performance decay post-publication.
known_outcome: Mixed — strategy-dependent. Some strategies (trend-following CTAs, statistical arbitrage in liquid markets) maintained alpha through 2020. However, Arnott et al. (2019, Financial Analysts Journal) documented widespread factor decay post-publication. The 2018-2019 quant quake showed factor crowding causing simultaneous drawdowns across funds. Cliff Asness (AQR) acknowledged alpha compression from 2018-2022. Persistence of alpha for top-decile quant managers is documented but not generalizable across the quant universe.
ground_truth_notes: UNRESOLVED captures the genuine heterogeneity: some quant strategies do generate persistent alpha (Renaissance Medallion is an extreme but real example) while most do not after fees and factor adjustment. The replication problem in factor research (Harvey et al. 2016) and the difficulty of distinguishing luck from skill in short track records make this genuinely unresolved at the 2010 evidence level. CI excludes zero for some strategies but not the general claim.
sources: Jegadeesh & Titman 1993, Journal of Finance — Returns to Buying Winners and Selling Losers
Harvey, Liu & Zhu 2016, Review of Financial Studies — ...and the Cross-Section of Expected Returns
Arnott et al. 2019, Financial Analysts Journal — Reports of Value's Death May Be Greatly Exaggerated
Zuckerman 2019, The Man Who Solved the Market (Renaissance Technologies data)

hypothesis: Financial prediction markets (e.g., Intrade, PredictIt) produce probability estimates that are more accurate than expert surveys and traditional polls for political and economic events.
evidence_available: By 2008, Intrade had operated for several electoral cycles with a track record. Berg et al. (2008, Journal of Economic Perspectives) documented the Iowa Electronic Markets outperforming polls in 451 of 596 comparisons from 1988-2004. Wolfers & Zitzewitz (2004, Journal of Economic Perspectives) provided theoretical and empirical support for prediction market efficiency. Arrow et al. (2008, Science) — signed by major economists — endorsed prediction markets. Tetlock (2005, Expert Political Judgment) had documented expert underperformance vs. simple models. Danish and German prediction market data provided cross-country replication. Markets showed reliable calibration on sports, entertainment, and political events.
known_outcome: Generally confirmed with important qualifications. Intrade successfully called 49/50 states in 2008 and 50/50 in 2012. Tetlock's Good Judgment Project (2011+, formalized in Superforecasting 2015) showed trained forecasters beat prediction markets in some domains. Prediction markets failed notably on Brexit (2016, gave 25% probability to Leave on election morning) and Trump 2016 (Predictwise gave Clinton 89% on election day). Supra-forecasters in structured tournaments outperformed unstructured markets. Overall accuracy advantage over polls confirmed but magnitude debated.
ground_truth_notes: GO is warranted at the 2008 evidence level: multiple cycles of data, cross-country replication, theoretical grounding, and CI excluding zero for the accuracy advantage over polls. The subsequent failures (Brexit, Trump 2016) involved thin liquidity and manipulation concerns that were not central to the 2008 evidence base. The hypothesis as stated — markets beat polls — had sufficient evidence to confirm.
sources: Berg, Nelson & Rietz 2008, Journal of Economic Perspectives — Prediction Market Accuracy in the Long Run
Wolfers & Zitzewitz 2004, Journal of Economic Perspectives — Prediction Markets
Arrow et al. 2008, Science — The Promise of Prediction Markets
Tetlock 2005, Expert Political Judgment (Princeton University Press)
Tetlock & Gardner 2015, Superforecasting (Crown Publishers)
